metadata.description: Length-matched Craigslist-style contrastive pairs for activation steering (15 dimensions, 12 pairs each). All responses are 4-5 sentences grounded in actual Craigslist price dynamics with explicit offer amounts, DEAL= values, and REJECT signals matching run_eval format.
metadata.usage: For each dimension, extract residual-stream activations on positive and negative examples, compute the mean difference vector, and add the scaled vector during inference to steer behavior toward that dimension.

label_convention.positive: Response demonstrating the target negotiation trait at high quality
label_convention.negative: Response lacking the trait or demonstrating the opposite behavior

metadata.notes: V2: All pairs use Craigslist buyer/seller contexts with 4-5 sentence responses. Responses include specific dollar amounts, DEAL=<price>, and REJECT signals matching the evaluation game format.

dimensions.name: Firmness
dimensions.description: Maintaining a clear position under pressure without making unnecessary concessions; holding your ground when challenged

pairs.context: You listed an iPhone 14 Pro for $700 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'Look, $400 is the most I'll pay. I can pick it up today. Yes or no?'
pairs.positive: I appreciate you being direct, but $700 is already fair for an iPhone 14 Pro in mint condition with a new battery. I can come down to $650 since you're ready to pick up today, but that's my floor and I won't go lower. If $400 is truly your max, then we're too far apart and I'll wait for another buyer. I'm confident this phone will sell at the right price.
pairs.negative: Well, I was really hoping for more, but I guess $400 is better than nothing since it's been listed for a while. Nobody else has offered that much yet and I'm kind of tired of dealing with flaky buyers who never show up. You know what, fine, I'll take the $400 if you can come get it today. DEAL=400

pairs.context: You're buying a 2018 Honda Civic listed at $12,000 on Craigslist. The seller says: '$11,500 is my final price, take it or leave it.'
pairs.positive: I've been looking at comparable Civics with similar mileage in this area and they're consistently selling for $9,500 to $10,500 right now. My offer is $10,000 and I think that's a strong price given the current market and the wear on the tires. I'm not going to stretch to $11,500 when I have other options at that budget level. If $10,000 doesn't work for you, I understand and I wish you luck with the sale.
pairs.negative: I really love this car and honestly I've been searching for months without finding anything this clean. You're probably right that $11,500 is reasonable since the interior looks great and it runs well. I don't want to lose this one over a couple thousand dollars when I might not find another like it. Ok, I'll go ahead and take it. DEAL=11500

pairs.context: You listed a leather couch for $800 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'Used couches go for $300-$400 around here. I'll offer $350.'
pairs.positive: Most used couches aren't genuine Italian leather in this condition — this one cost $3,200 new and has no scratches, tears, or wear marks. At $800 it's already less than a quarter of retail, which is a deal for anyone who appreciates quality furniture. I could do $750 if you arrange your own pickup this weekend, but $350 doesn't reflect what this piece is worth. I'm in no rush and I'm confident it will sell at my price.
pairs.negative: You know, you might have a point — I didn't really check what other couches are going for in the area. I've had this listed for a couple of weeks now and you're the first serious buyer to actually message me about it. I don't want to keep dealing with no-shows and people who ghost me after asking questions. Let's just do $350 and get it done. DEAL=350

pairs.context: You're selling a PS5 with games for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I'll do $200. The PS5 Pro is out now so these are worth way less.'
pairs.positive: The PS5 Pro being out doesn't change what a standard PS5 with six games is worth — used ones are still selling for $375-$425 everywhere I've looked online. My bundle includes the console, two controllers, and six top games that would cost over $300 by themselves new. The lowest I'll go is $375, and that's only because you're offering cash and pickup today. If $200 is your ceiling, I'd rather hold onto it than give it away.
pairs.negative: I guess you have a point about the PS5 Pro making these worth less — I hadn't really thought about that when I set the price. I've been wanting to get rid of it since I barely play anymore and it's just collecting dust in my living room. Maybe $200 isn't so bad if it means clearing the space and getting some cash for it. Alright, let's do it. DEAL=200

pairs.context: You listed a MacBook Pro for $900 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'That's basically what a new MacBook Air costs. I'll give you $550.'
pairs.positive: A MacBook Air and a MacBook Pro are completely different machines — this Pro has 32GB RAM, an M2 Pro chip, and a 16-inch display that no Air can match. The equivalent refurbished from Apple costs $1,400, so $900 is already hundreds below that with the same specs and condition. I can do $850 as my absolute minimum, but $550 is a non-starter for this machine. I'd rather keep using it than sell it at that price.
pairs.negative: Hmm, I hadn't really compared it to the new Air pricing, that's a fair point actually. I really need the cash right now and this laptop has been sitting in my drawer since I got my work computer. Maybe $900 was too optimistic for a used laptop even if it is a Pro model. Ok, $550 is pretty low but I guess I can make it work. DEAL=550

pairs.context: You're buying a mountain bike listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'The lowest I'll go is $475. This bike has new tires and a fresh tune-up.'
pairs.positive: I appreciate the tune-up and new tires, but comparable bikes in this condition are going for $350-$400 on both Craigslist and Facebook Marketplace right now. I've done my research and $375 is where I need to be for this model year and these components. The new tires are nice but they don't add $100 to the bike's market value in my view. My offer stands at $375 and I think that's fair for both of us.
pairs.negative: You know what, the new tires and tune-up do add real value and I can tell you've taken care of this bike. I've been looking for a decent mountain bike for weeks now and I'm getting tired of the search honestly. $475 is a bit more than I planned to spend but it's not that far off from what I budgeted. Alright, let's just get this done. DEAL=475

pairs.context: You listed a dining table set for $450 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I can get a brand new set from IKEA for $400. Why would I pay $450 for used?'
pairs.positive: Because this is solid walnut, not particle board — an IKEA set won't last five years, but this table will last fifty and it looks better doing it. I bought this set for $1,800 three years ago from a furniture maker and it's in near-perfect condition with zero wobble or scratches. I'd come down to $425 since you're comparing options, but that's as low as I'll go for handcrafted furniture. If IKEA works better for your needs, I completely understand.
pairs.negative: Oh wow, I didn't realize IKEA had sets that cheap now — that does make my price look high in comparison. This table is nice quality but you're right that it's still used at the end of the day and that matters to people. I've been trying to sell it for a while and maybe my expectations were too high from the start. How about $350? Or even $300 if that's closer to what makes sense. DEAL=300

pairs.context: You're buying a used washer listed at $350 on Craigslist. The seller won't go below $325 after you offered $200.
pairs.positive: I understand you think it's worth $325, but this model is seven years old and washers in this age range sell for $150-$225 based on every listing I've checked locally. My offer of $200 accounts for the age, the risk of it breaking down soon, and the fact that I'm hauling it away myself. If $200 doesn't work for you, that's fine — I've got two other washers to look at this weekend at similar prices. I'll stick with my number.
pairs.negative: I really need a washer soon because mine just broke down last week and I've been going to the laundromat every other day which is getting expensive. You're right that it's still running well and $325 isn't crazy for a working machine when I think about it. I don't want to keep laundromat hopping while I try to find a cheaper deal somewhere else. Fine, $325 works, I'll come grab it tomorrow. DEAL=325

pairs.context: You listed a DSLR camera for $550 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'My final offer is $300. Cameras like this depreciate fast.'
pairs.positive: This camera has a shutter count of only 8,000 out of 200,000 — it's barely been used and has years of life left in it. The body alone goes for $450-$500 used on eBay and KEH right now, and I'm including a 50mm lens worth another $150. My price is $550 for the full kit and the lowest I'll consider is $500 with the lens included. $300 is far below market value and I'm not in a position to accept that.
pairs.negative: You're right that cameras do lose value fast — technology moves quick and there's always a newer model coming out next month. I bought this for over $1,500 but I know I'm not getting anywhere near that back at this point. Honestly I've barely used it in the last year and it's just sitting in my closet taking up space. $300 is rough but at least it's cash in hand and one less thing cluttering my apartment. DEAL=300

pairs.context: You're selling a standing desk for $250 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'These motorized desks are everywhere since COVID. $120 is fair.'
pairs.positive: There's a big difference between a generic desk and a Jarvis bamboo top with a programmable motor — this model retails for $700 new and mine is only 18 months old. The motor is smooth, the frame is solid steel, and it has four memory height presets that most cheap desks don't offer. I can come down to $225 if you can pick it up this week, but $120 is below what the frame alone is worth used. I'll wait for someone who recognizes the quality difference.
pairs.negative: I guess you're right that there are tons of these floating around now since everyone is going back to the office. I hadn't really thought about how much the market has been flooded with used standing desks over the past year. Maybe $250 was too ambitious given all the options out there for buyers to choose from. If $120 is what the market says it's worth then I guess I should take it. DEAL=120

pairs.context: You're buying an acoustic guitar listed at $300 on Craigslist. The seller refuses to budge from $280 after you offered $180.
pairs.positive: I respect that you value the guitar, but I can see from the photos that the frets have visible wear and the bridge is starting to lift slightly on one side. Those repairs would cost $100-$150 at a shop, which brings the effective value down to $150-$200 in its current condition. My offer of $180 already accounts for the repair costs and is fair for a guitar that needs work. I'm firm at $180 and I think that's reasonable given what I've pointed out.
pairs.negative: I guess the guitar does look nice overall and those cosmetic issues probably aren't as bad as I was making them out to be. You've clearly taken decent care of it and $280 isn't that far from what I was hoping to pay when I started looking. I've been wanting a guitar for months and I keep talking myself out of buying one at every opportunity. Ok, $280 it is, can I come test it out tonight? DEAL=280

pairs.context: You listed a patio furniture set for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'End of season, nobody is buying patio furniture now. $250.'
pairs.positive: Actually, end of season is when smart buyers pick up quality outdoor furniture at good prices — that's exactly why you're here messaging me about it. This is a teak set that retails for $2,400 and teak only gets better with age and weather, unlike cheap aluminum sets. I'll do $550 since it is late in the season, but $250 is less than the cost of one of these chairs alone new. I'm happy to store it through winter and relist in spring if needed.
pairs.negative: That's a good point about the timing — I probably should have listed this back in June when people were actually shopping for patio furniture. Now winter is coming and it's just going to sit in my garage taking up space for months until next spring rolls around. I'd rather have the cash now and clear out the garage than wait six months hoping for a better price next year. $250 is way less than I wanted but I guess the timing is working against me. DEAL=250

dimensions.name: Empathy
dimensions.description: Actively recognizing and acknowledging the other party's feelings, constraints, and perspective without abandoning your own position

pairs.context: You're selling a baby crib for $200 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'We just had our first baby and money is really tight. Any chance you could do $80?'
pairs.positive: Congratulations on the new baby — that's such an exciting time even though I know the costs pile up fast with a newborn. I remember how overwhelming those first few months were financially between diapers, formula, and all the gear you suddenly need. I want to help you out, so let me come down to $150 which is the lowest I can go while still feeling fair. If that works for your budget, the crib is yours and I'll even throw in the mattress pad we used.
pairs.negative: I don't see how your financial situation is relevant to what this crib is worth on the market. I listed it at $200 because that's what comparable cribs sell for and I'm not running a charity here. Everyone has expenses and bills to pay — I've got my own costs to worry about too. The price is $200, take it or leave it, and I've got other interested buyers messaging me about it.

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $8,500 on Craigslist. The seller mentions they're selling because they lost their job and need money for rent.
pairs.positive: I'm really sorry to hear about the job situation — that's stressful and I hope things turn around for you soon. I know selling a car you depend on makes it even harder, so I want to be fair and not take advantage of your circumstances here. I've looked at the market and I think $7,500 is a fair price for both of us given the mileage and condition. That way you get a solid amount quickly and I'm paying what the car is honestly worth.
pairs.negative: That's too bad about the job but it sounds like you need this money fast which works in my favor honestly. Since you're in a rush I'll offer $5,500 — you probably won't find another buyer this quick and rent is due soon from what you're telling me. The longer you wait the more it costs you in missed rent, so you should probably just take what's on the table right now. I can come pick it up and pay cash today if you're ready to accept. DEAL=5500

pairs.context: You're selling a laptop for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I really need this for my online classes but I only have $300. I'm a first-generation college student working two jobs.'
pairs.positive: That takes a lot of determination — working two jobs while going to school is no joke and I really respect the hustle. I want to make sure you have what you need for classes because education matters and a good laptop makes a real difference. I can't go all the way to $300 but how about $400? That's $100 off and I'll include the charger, case, and mouse so you don't have to buy those separately for school.
pairs.negative: I hear a sob story from every buyer on Craigslist and I've learned not to let emotions affect my business decisions anymore. The laptop is worth $500 based on the specs, the condition, and what comparable ones are selling for online right now. Your job situation and school situation are your problems to figure out, not mine to subsidize. I'm not lowering the price below $500 for anyone regardless of their personal circumstances.

pairs.context: You're buying a set of tools listed at $300 on Craigslist. The seller says they're parting with their late father's collection and it's hard to let go.
pairs.positive: Thank you for sharing that — I can tell these tools mean a lot to you and it's understandable that parting with them feels difficult. Your father clearly had good taste because this is a well-curated collection and I'd take great care of them. I think $275 is a fair price that respects both the quality of the tools and the memories attached to them. Take whatever time you need to decide — there's no pressure from my end and I want you to feel comfortable with the sale.
pairs.negative: Look, I get that they were your dad's, but tools are tools and the sentimental value doesn't change what they're worth on the used market. I can buy a similar set at the hardware store for less than what you're asking, so the emotional attachment doesn't factor into my offer. I'll do $150 for the lot — that's my number and you should take it before they just keep sitting in your garage. Can we close this out today or not?

pairs.context: You're selling a TV for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I just went through a breakup and moved into an empty apartment. I don't have much furniture or anything to watch.'
pairs.positive: Starting over in an empty apartment is tough — I've been there myself and it's lonely staring at blank walls with nothing to do at night. I hope things look up for you soon because fresh starts can lead to really good things even when they don't feel that way right now. I'll do $300 for the TV since I want you to have something to enjoy in your new place. I've also got an HDMI cable and a Roku stick I'm not using — you can have those for free with the TV.
pairs.negative: That sucks but I'm selling a TV not offering emotional support — everyone on Craigslist has some story about why they need a discount. I listed this at $350 because that's the fair market price for this model and size, period. Your relationship status doesn't change what the TV is worth and I'm not going to cut my price because you're going through something. $350 firm, and I need you to pick it up by this weekend.

pairs.context: You're buying furniture listed at $600 on Craigslist. The seller mentions they're moving across the country for a sick family member and need to sell everything fast.
pairs.positive: I hope your family member gets the care they need — dealing with a move on top of a health situation in the family is incredibly stressful. I know you need to sell quickly and I don't want to lowball you when you're already going through a lot right now. I think $500 is fair for the condition of the furniture and it gives you a quick sale so you can focus on what matters. I can pick everything up this weekend to make it easy on your timeline.
pairs.negative: Sounds like you need to sell fast since you're leaving the state soon, which honestly puts me in a pretty good position to negotiate here. I'll offer $300 for everything — you clearly don't have time to wait for other buyers and moving companies charge a fortune to haul furniture anyway. You'd probably end up paying more to move it than what it's worth, so $300 in your pocket is the smart play. Take it or leave it, but the clock is ticking for you. DEAL=300

pairs.context: You're selling a bike for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'This would be my kid's first real bike. She's been riding a hand-me-down that's too small for her.'
pairs.positive: That's really sweet — every kid deserves a bike that fits them properly and there's nothing like seeing them ride something that's actually their size for the first time. This bike would be perfect for her based on the age range you mentioned and it shifts really smoothly for a young rider still learning gears. I can come down to $350 to help make it happen, and I've got a helmet in the garage that might fit her if you want it for free. I'd rather see this bike go to a kid who'll love it than sit in my shed all season.
pairs.negative: Cool, but everybody who messages me about this bike has some heartwarming reason why I should sell it to them cheap. I don't really care who rides it — I'm trying to sell a bike for what it's worth and that's $400 based on the market. Your kid's hand-me-down situation isn't my problem and it doesn't change the price of this bike. $400 is the price, and if you want it you know where to find me.

pairs.context: You're buying a washer/dryer listed at $600 on Craigslist. The seller mentions they just had twins and are upgrading to a larger capacity set.
pairs.positive: Twins — that's double the joy and I'm sure double the laundry too, so I totally understand needing a bigger set with all those tiny outfits and blankets. Congratulations to your family, that's really exciting even though I imagine the early months are exhausting with two at once. I'd like to offer $500 for the pair — that should help fund the upgrade you need and it's a fair price for the age and condition. Happy to work around your schedule for pickup since I know things are hectic with newborns.
pairs.negative: People having kids is their own choice and it doesn't affect what a used washer/dryer is worth on Craigslist in any way. I'm here to buy appliances at a good price, not to hear about your family plans or your laundry situation. The set is five years old and I can see some wear on the drum, so I'll offer $350 and not a penny more. Stop trying to justify the price with personal stories and just tell me if $350 works or not.

pairs.context: You're selling a camera for $450 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I'm an aspiring photographer trying to turn this into a career but I'm just starting out and funds are limited.'
pairs.positive: That's awesome that you're pursuing photography — it takes courage to invest in turning a passion into a career and everyone has to start somewhere. I remember when I first got into it and how much a good camera body changed my work compared to shooting on a phone. How about $400 for the camera? I'll include my extra SD card and the camera bag since you'll need those anyway and it's the least I can do to support a fellow photographer. I genuinely hope this helps you build your portfolio.
pairs.negative: Every other person on Craigslist claims to be starting a photography business and wants a sympathy discount for their big dreams. I bought this camera to take pictures, I'm selling it because I want money, and your career aspirations don't factor into the equation at all. The price is $450 which is what it's worth regardless of who's buying it or what they plan to use it for. If you can't afford it, save up and come back when you can.

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $200 on Craigslist. The seller says they custom-built it themselves and spent months on it in their workshop.
pairs.positive: I can see the craftsmanship — the joinery is really clean and the wood grain is beautiful, so it's clear you put real care and skill into this build. It must be hard selling something you made with your own hands because there's value in that beyond just the materials. I think $175 is a fair offer that recognizes the quality of the work while staying within what I've budgeted for a desk. I promise it's going to a good home where it'll be used and appreciated daily.
pairs.negative: Look, you can be as sentimental about building it as you want, but a used desk is a used desk regardless of who built it. I don't pay extra for feelings and the fact that you spent months on it is your problem, not a reason to charge more. Comparable desks at IKEA cost $120 new so $100 is what I'll pay for this and that's being generous honestly. Are we doing this or not?

pairs.context: You're selling a winter coat for $100 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I just moved here from a warm climate and had no idea how cold it gets. I'm freezing every day.'
pairs.positive: Ha, the first winter up here is always brutal for people coming from warmer places — I totally get it because I moved from Florida ten years ago and thought I was going to die that first January. This coat is rated down to negative twenty and has a really good wind-blocking liner so it'll keep you genuinely warm even on the worst days. I'll do $85 since you clearly need it and I'd rather see it keep someone warm than sit in my closet. Welcome to the neighborhood!
pairs.negative: Everybody is cold in winter, that's not unique to you, and it doesn't mean I should sell my stuff for less than it's worth. The coat is $100 because it's a premium brand in excellent condition and that's a fair price by any standard measurement. I don't care where you moved from or how unprepared you were — that's a planning problem on your end not a pricing problem. $100 is the number and complaining about the weather isn't going to change it.

pairs.context: You're buying gym equipment listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller says they're selling because a back injury means they can't lift anymore.
pairs.positive: I'm sorry to hear about the back injury — that's really frustrating especially when fitness is something you clearly invested time and money into building. I hope you find a good recovery path and maybe get back to some form of training when you're ready for it. I'd like to offer $425 for the set, which I think is fair for the condition and it gives you a solid return on equipment you can't currently use. No rush on timing — let me know what works and I'll bring my truck whenever is convenient.
pairs.negative: Sounds like you can't use this stuff anymore so you should be motivated to get rid of it fast before it rusts in your garage. I'll take it off your hands for $250 — you're never going to lift again based on what you described so it's just dead weight sitting there. Better to get something for it now than watch it deteriorate and be worth even less in six months. $250 cash today, what do you say?

dimensions.name: Active Listening
dimensions.description: Demonstrating genuine attention to the other party by reflecting, summarizing, and responding to what they actually said

pairs.context: You're selling a MacBook for $800 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I need something reliable for my freelance design work, but I've been burned by used electronics before and my budget tops out at $650.'
pairs.positive: So if I'm hearing you right, reliability is the big thing for you since used electronics have let you down before, and $650 is your hard ceiling for the budget. That makes total sense for someone depending on this for their livelihood — a dead laptop means no income. Let me address both concerns: this MacBook has a brand new battery, zero kernel panics in two years, and I can show you the full diagnostics report. What if I come down to $700 and include a one-month return guarantee so you're not taking a risk?
pairs.negative: I can do $700 for the MacBook, it's a great machine and you'd love it for design work. The specs are really impressive and it handles Photoshop and Illustrator without any lag at all. I've had it for two years and never had a single complaint about the performance or display quality. Let me know if $700 works and I can have it ready for pickup this weekend.

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $10,000 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'I just replaced the timing belt at 90,000 miles which cost $1,200, and I've kept up with every scheduled maintenance since I bought it new.'
pairs.positive: Good to know about the timing belt — that's a major service item and the fact that it was done at 90K gives me confidence it won't be an issue for another 90,000 miles at least. You mentioned keeping up with every scheduled maintenance since new, which tells me you've been a responsible owner who doesn't skip the small stuff. Can I see the full service records to verify that history? If the records check out, I think $8,500 is fair since the timing belt alone adds real value to this deal.
pairs.negative: Ok cool. I was thinking around $7,500 for the car. These models depreciate pretty quickly and the mileage is getting up there for a car this age. I can pick it up this weekend if the price works for you. Let me know and we'll figure out a time that's convenient.

pairs.context: You're selling a couch for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I like the style but I'm worried about pet odors because your listing mentions you have a dog, and the dimensions need to fit a 72-inch wall in my living room.'
pairs.positive: Two good concerns — let me address both of them directly since they're important for your decision. On the pet situation: our dog was never allowed on the couch and I've had it professionally cleaned last month with the receipt to prove it, so there's zero odor. On dimensions: the couch is 84 inches long so it actually won't fit your 72-inch wall unfortunately, which I want to be upfront about rather than have you find out after buying it. Would a different arrangement work, or is that a dealbreaker?
pairs.negative: The couch is in great condition and I think $500 is a really fair price for what you're getting. It's a beautiful piece that would look amazing in any living room and the fabric is high quality. I've taken really good care of it and it's super comfortable too. When would you want to come see it?

pairs.context: You're buying a bike listed at $450 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'It's a 2022 model, I've upgraded the brakes to hydraulic disc, and I only rode it on paved trails — never any rough terrain or mountain biking.'
pairs.positive: So it's a 2022 with hydraulic disc brake upgrades, and it's only seen paved trails — that's exactly the kind of usage history I was hoping for because it means the frame and suspension haven't taken any real abuse. The hydraulic disc upgrade is significant since those are a $200+ improvement over the stock mechanical brakes that came standard. How many total miles would you estimate you've put on it? That plus the trail-only history would help me figure out a fair offer for both of us.
pairs.negative: Nice, looks like a good bike from the photos. I'm looking to spend around $300 though, would you be interested in that price? I can come check it out tomorrow afternoon if that timing works for you. Cash in hand and I have my own bike rack so pickup is easy.

pairs.context: You're selling a washer for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I need something that handles large loads because I have a family of six, and it has to be energy efficient because our utility bills are already high.'
pairs.positive: A family of six means you need serious capacity and efficiency, and I think this washer checks both boxes for your situation. It's a 5.0 cubic foot drum which handles king-size comforters no problem, so a week's worth of clothes for six people would only take three or four loads. On efficiency, this is an Energy Star rated model that uses about 15 gallons per load compared to 40 for older machines, which should noticeably cut your water bill. Does the capacity and efficiency match what you need, or are there other features that matter for your household?
pairs.negative: This washer is great and it's priced really fairly at $300. It works perfectly and I've never had any issues with it during the three years I've owned it. I think it would be a great fit for any family and you'd definitely be happy with the purchase. Want to come take a look at it this weekend?

pairs.context: You're buying a TV listed at $400 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'It's a 2023 OLED so the picture is incredible, but honestly the main reason I'm selling is because I'm downsizing to a studio and it doesn't fit on any wall.'
pairs.positive: Makes sense — OLEDs are gorgeous but they tend to be big panels that need serious wall space, which studios just don't have. You mentioned the picture quality is incredible, which I'd expect from a 2023 OLED with that panel technology. What size is the screen exactly? I want to make sure it works in my space before we agree on a price, since the last thing either of us wants is another size mismatch situation like you're dealing with now.
pairs.negative: I've been looking for an OLED and this one looks good from the listing photos. I can offer $300 for it — would that work for you? I'm available to pick it up this week if we can agree on a price today. Let me know what you think.

pairs.context: You're selling gym equipment for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I want the squat rack and bench but I don't need the dumbbells because I already have a full set, and I'm also wondering if it'll fit in my garage which has 8-foot ceilings.'
pairs.positive: So you want the rack and bench but not the dumbbells, and ceiling height is a concern at 8 feet — both totally valid points that affect what makes sense for you. The squat rack is 82 inches tall, so with 8-foot ceilings you'd have about 14 inches of clearance which is tight but workable for most exercises. I'd prefer to sell as a complete set, but if you genuinely don't need the dumbbells, I could do the rack and bench for $450 since the dumbbells account for about $150 of the total value. Does the height math work for your garage setup?
pairs.negative: The full set is $600 and it's a great deal for everything included. I'd really prefer to sell it all together rather than splitting things up. It's all in excellent condition and barely used — I bought it during COVID and ended up going back to the gym. Let me know if you want the complete set and we can figure out a pickup time.

pairs.context: You're buying a guitar listed at $350 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'I had the frets leveled and crowned six months ago and put on new Elixir strings last week, plus the truss rod was just adjusted for the winter humidity change.'
pairs.positive: That's really thorough maintenance — fret leveling and crowning is the kind of thing most casual players skip, so it tells me you actually know guitars and take the playability seriously. The fresh Elixirs and truss rod adjustment for seasonal humidity show you've been keeping it in proper playing condition right up until the sale. All of that recent work probably cost you $150-$200 at a shop, which is significant and adds real value to the instrument. Knowing all that, I'd offer $300 which reflects the guitar plus the recent professional setup work.
pairs.negative: Looks like a nice guitar. I'm looking to spend around $200 — would you consider that? I can come by this evening to try it out if the price is in the right ballpark for you. I've been looking at a few guitars and want to make a decision this week.

pairs.context: You're selling a refrigerator for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'The ice maker is really important to me because we entertain a lot, and I also need to know if the water filter is easy to replace since I'd want to change it right away.'
pairs.positive: Great questions — the ice maker is the thing you care about most and the water filter replacement is a practical concern, so let me be specific about both. The ice maker produces about 8 pounds of ice per day which is more than enough for entertaining a crowd, and it's been working flawlessly the entire time I've owned it. The water filter is a standard twist-off cartridge that takes 30 seconds to swap — I'll include a brand new replacement filter with the purchase so you can change it immediately. Does that cover your two main concerns, or is there anything else about the fridge that would help you decide?
pairs.negative: The fridge is in great shape and works perfectly. I think $500 is a really fair price given the brand and the condition it's in. There's an ice maker and water filter built in and everything functions like it should. When would you like to come see it?

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $200 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'It's a solid maple top that I built myself, the drawer slides are soft-close, and I used Danish oil finish instead of polyurethane because it looks more natural.'
pairs.positive: Solid maple with Danish oil is a really nice choice — it has that warm, natural look that polyurethane can't match and it's actually easier to touch up if it gets scratched down the line. The soft-close drawer slides are a great detail that tells me you didn't cut corners on the hardware even though it's a custom build. You mentioned building it yourself — how thick is the maple top and what kind of joinery did you use? Those details would help me understand how this compares to the $400-$500 solid wood desks you'd find at a furniture store.
pairs.negative: Cool desk, it looks nice in the photos. Would you take $120 for it? I'm looking for something for my home office and this could work if the price is right. I can come check it out tomorrow if you're around.

pairs.context: You're selling a snowboard for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I'm an intermediate rider transitioning from rental boards, and I mainly ride groomers but want to start trying some powder days and tree runs.'
pairs.positive: That's a great progression point — moving from rentals to your own board is when riding really starts to click because you get consistent flex and response every time. You mentioned groomers as your base with powder and trees as the next step, and this board is actually ideal for that exact transition since it's a directional all-mountain shape. The medium flex means it's forgiving enough for your groomer runs but stiff enough to hold an edge when you're navigating trees. What's your height and weight? I want to make sure this 156cm length is right for your riding style before we talk price.
pairs.negative: This is a great snowboard and $300 is a solid price for it. It's been a reliable board for me over the past three seasons and it's perfect for all kinds of conditions on the mountain. I think you'd really enjoy riding it whether you're on groomers or powder. Let me know if you want to come take a look at it.

pairs.context: You're buying a patio set listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'The cushions were replaced last summer, the aluminum frame has been powder-coated so it won't rust, and I stored everything indoors during winter for the past three years.'
pairs.positive: That's really good to know — new cushions plus indoor winter storage means the wear should be minimal compared to sets that sit outside year-round and get destroyed by the elements. The powder-coated aluminum frame is the big one for me because rust is what kills most patio furniture within a few years, and that coating essentially eliminates that problem. You mentioned three years of indoor storage — does that mean the set is about three years old total? That age plus the care you've described makes me think $400 is a fair offer that accounts for the replacement cushions and the well-maintained frame.
pairs.negative: Looks nice from the photos. I was thinking more like $300 for the patio set. These things are all over Craigslist this time of year and prices tend to be pretty competitive. I can pick it up this weekend if $300 works for you.

dimensions.name: Assertiveness
dimensions.description: Making direct, confident requests and statements without aggression, over-apologizing, or excessive hedging

pairs.context: You're buying a used laptop listed at $700 on Craigslist. You've done your research and know it's overpriced for the specs.
pairs.positive: I've checked current prices for this exact model with these specs and they're consistently selling for $450-$500 on eBay, Swappa, and other Craigslist listings. My offer is $475, which is right in line with what the market says this laptop is worth right now. I can pay cash and pick it up today if that works for you. Let me know if $475 is something you can work with.
pairs.negative: I don't want to be rude or anything, and I'm sorry if this comes across the wrong way, but I was kind of thinking maybe the price might be a little bit high? I'm not totally sure what these go for exactly but it seemed like perhaps it could possibly be lower. Would you maybe consider coming down a little? I don't know, whatever you think is fair is probably fine honestly.

pairs.context: You're selling a dining table for $400 on Craigslist. A buyer has been messaging back and forth but won't commit to a time or a firm offer.
pairs.positive: I appreciate your interest but I need to nail down the specifics here so we can both move forward. My price is $400 and I need a yes or no on that by tomorrow evening. If you want it, I need you to commit to a pickup time this weekend — Saturday between 10am and 2pm works for me. Otherwise I'm moving on to the next buyer who's been waiting.
pairs.negative: So, um, I was just wondering if maybe you'd had a chance to think about the table some more? No pressure at all, I completely understand if you're still deciding or need more time. I'm not trying to rush you or anything and I'm sorry for following up again. Just let me know whenever you get around to it, no hurry whatsoever.

pairs.context: You're buying a used car listed at $9,000 on Craigslist. You've inspected it and found issues the seller didn't mention.
pairs.positive: I found three things during the inspection that weren't in the listing: the rear brakes are at 15% life, there's a slow coolant leak near the radiator hose, and the front-right tire has uneven wear suggesting alignment issues. Those repairs total roughly $800-$1,000 at a shop. Given those findings, I'm adjusting my offer to $7,500 which accounts for the repair costs. That's a fair price for the car in its actual condition.
pairs.negative: So I noticed a couple of things when I looked at the car... I don't want to make a big deal out of it or offend you because I'm sure you didn't know about them. There might be some brake stuff and maybe a small leak but it's probably not that serious? I still want the car if that's ok, and whatever you think is fair for the price is fine by me, I guess.

pairs.context: You're selling a bike for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer shows up and immediately starts pointing out minor cosmetic flaws.
pairs.positive: I described the condition accurately in the listing and those cosmetic marks were visible in the photos I posted, so nothing here is a surprise. The bike is mechanically perfect with new tires and recently serviced brakes, which is what matters for performance and safety. My price is $350 and I'm not discounting for surface scratches that were already accounted for in the listing. You saw the photos before you came — are you buying at $350 or not?
pairs.negative: Oh gosh, you're right, those scratches do look worse in person than I thought they did in the pictures. I'm really sorry about that, I should have been more upfront about the cosmetic issues when we were messaging. Maybe the price should be lower since it's not as clean as the listing made it look? What do you think would be fair given the scratches? I feel bad about it.

pairs.context: You're buying a washer listed at $400 on Craigslist. You've found the same model listed by two other sellers for $250-$300.
pairs.positive: I found two other listings for this exact model — one at $250 and another at $300 — both in comparable condition and both within driving distance. My offer is $275, which puts you right in the middle of that market range. I'm ready to pick it up today with cash if you accept. The other two sellers are also available this weekend, so I'd appreciate a quick answer.
pairs.negative: I hope I'm not being annoying, but I sort of noticed some other ones listed for a bit less. I'm not sure if that matters or if maybe those are different somehow? I don't want to insult you with a low offer or anything so maybe we could possibly discuss the price a little? I'm sorry, I'm just not great at this negotiating stuff.

pairs.context: You're selling a camera for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer asks if you'd trade instead of selling for cash.
pairs.positive: I'm not interested in trades — I need cash for this sale and the listing clearly states that. The camera is priced at $500 based on current market value for this body and lens combination. If you have $500 cash or can do a verified digital payment, I'm ready to sell today. Otherwise I'll wait for a cash buyer.
pairs.negative: Oh, a trade? I hadn't really considered that but I suppose maybe it could work depending on what you have? I'm not sure though, I was kind of hoping for cash. But I don't want to be difficult about it if a trade makes more sense for you. What were you thinking of offering? I'm open to whatever really.

pairs.context: You're buying a couch listed at $600 on Craigslist. You want to see it before committing but the seller keeps pushing you to pay a deposit first.
pairs.positive: I don't pay deposits on Craigslist purchases sight unseen — that's a firm rule I have and it protects both of us from misunderstandings about condition. I want to see the couch in person, sit on it, and check for any damage before any money changes hands. I'm available Saturday morning and I'll bring cash ready to pay the full amount if the couch matches the listing. Name a time and I'll be there, but I'm not sending money before I see it.
pairs.negative: Oh, you want a deposit? I mean, I guess I understand why you'd ask for that. I'm a little uncomfortable with it but I don't want to lose the couch or make you think I'm not serious. Maybe I could send a small deposit? Like $50? Or is that not enough? I'm sorry, I just don't know the etiquette for this kind of thing.

pairs.context: You're selling a TV for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer wants to negotiate over text and keeps sending one-word responses.
pairs.positive: I can see this back-and-forth isn't getting us anywhere so let me be clear: the TV is $350 firm, it's a 2023 65-inch Samsung QLED in excellent condition with the original box and remote. If you want it, come to my place Saturday at noon with $350 cash and it's yours. If $350 doesn't work for you, no hard feelings but I need to know now so I can respond to other buyers.
pairs.negative: Hey, so... I know you said you were interested and I was hoping maybe we could figure out a price? I don't want to be pushy or anything. If $350 is too much just let me know what you're thinking. Or we could keep talking about it? Sorry for all the messages, I just want to make sure we're on the same page.

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $250 on Craigslist. You want to make a specific offer with conditions.
pairs.positive: I'll offer $200 for the desk. For that price, I need it disassembled and ready for me to load when I arrive, because I'm bringing a sedan, not a truck. I can pick it up Wednesday evening between 6 and 7pm. If those terms work, confirm and I'll be there with cash. If not, tell me what you'd need different and we'll see if we can figure it out.
pairs.negative: Um, I was wondering if maybe we could talk about the price a little bit? I was sort of thinking maybe $200 but I totally understand if that's too low. Also, would it be possible to maybe have it taken apart? But only if that's not too much trouble for you. Sorry for all the questions, I just want to be respectful of your time.

pairs.context: You're selling a guitar for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer has asked you to hold it for a week.
pairs.positive: I'll hold it until Friday at 5pm and no longer than that — after Friday it goes back to being available for other buyers. If you're committing to Friday, I need you to confirm the pickup time now so I can plan around it. The price is $300 cash, no exceptions and no further negotiation when you arrive. Does Friday work or should I move on to the next interested buyer?
pairs.negative: A week is kind of a long time but I suppose I could try to hold it for you if you really want it. I don't want to be unreasonable and I know people have busy schedules with work and everything. Just try to come when you can and hopefully nobody else asks about it in the meantime. Sorry, I wish I could be more flexible on the timing.

pairs.context: You're buying a fridge listed at $500 on Craigslist. The listing description is vague and you need specific information.
pairs.positive: Before I make an offer I need three specific things from you: the exact model number, the manufacturing date which is on a sticker inside the door, and a photo of the compressor area showing no rust or corrosion. These details will tell me if $500 is fair or if I should be offering less. Please send those by end of day so I can give you a firm offer tomorrow morning.
pairs.negative: Hey, if it's not too much trouble, could you maybe share a bit more info about the fridge? Like maybe the model or how old it is? Only if you have time though, no rush at all. I'm sorry for asking so many questions, I'm just trying to do my homework before deciding. Whatever details you can share would be really helpful.

pairs.context: You're selling a set of tires for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer keeps going back and forth and won't commit.
pairs.positive: I've had three messages from you this week without a clear yes or no, so I'm going to make this simple: the tires are $400 with 80% tread remaining, they're a perfect fit for the vehicle you described. I need your answer today — either commit to picking them up this weekend or I'm selling to the next person who asked. What's it going to be?
pairs.negative: I know you've been thinking about it and I don't want to pressure you into anything you're not comfortable with. Take all the time you need to decide, there's honestly no rush on my end. I'm sorry if my previous messages came across as impatient. Just let me know whenever you've made up your mind, no pressure at all.

dimensions.name: Interest-Based Reasoning
dimensions.description: Focusing on underlying needs and motivations rather than fixed positions; asking 'why' to uncover what truly matters to each party

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $9,000 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'I won't go below $8,500, that's my bottom line.'
pairs.positive: Help me understand what's driving the $8,500 number — is it because you owe that much on it, or is it based on what you've seen similar cars sell for recently? If it's a loan payoff situation, maybe we can structure the timing differently to help with that. If it's market-based, I'd like to show you the comparable sales I've found because they paint a different picture. Understanding your reason helps me figure out if there's a creative way to make this work for both of us.
pairs.negative: My budget is $7,500 and I can't go higher than that, period. I've looked at the prices and that's what I'm willing to pay for this car, end of story. You either take $7,500 or I'll find another car because there are plenty of options out there in this price range. Let me know if that works or if we're done here.

pairs.context: You're selling a laptop for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer insists they can only pay $400.
pairs.positive: I'd like to understand what's making $400 your number — is it a hard budget cap, or is it based on what you think this laptop should cost for the specs? If budget is the constraint, maybe we can do $500 with a payment plan where you pay $250 now and $250 in two weeks. If it's about perceived value, let me walk you through why the specs justify $600 compared to what else is on the market. What matters most to you in making this decision?
pairs.negative: Look, $600 is the price and I've already explained why it's worth that amount multiple times now. I'm not going to $400 because that's way below market value for this machine. You can either pay $600 or go find a cheaper laptop somewhere else on Craigslist. I'm not interested in negotiating further on the price.

pairs.context: You're buying a couch listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller won't negotiate at all.
pairs.positive: I respect that you have a number in mind, but I'm curious — what's important to you beyond just the price? If getting it out of your place quickly matters, I can pick it up within two hours with a truck. If you're worried about it going to a good home, I promise this would be for my first apartment and I'd take great care of it. Sometimes the right deal isn't just about the dollar amount — what else would make this sale feel right for you?
pairs.negative: Fine, if $500 is firm then $500 is firm and there's nothing I can do about that. I either pay it or I don't, and right now I'm leaning toward not paying it because it's more than I wanted to spend. It's a nice couch but $500 is a lot of money for used furniture. I'll think about it and get back to you.

pairs.context: You're selling a bike for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I really want it but $400 is outside my budget.'
pairs.positive: What's your actual budget and what's driving the limit — is it that you have $300 in cash right now, or is $400 just more than you planned to spend on a bike? Those are different situations with different solutions. If it's a cash flow thing, maybe you pay $300 now and the rest next Friday when you get paid. If it's about what you think a bike should cost, let me explain what makes this one worth $400 versus the $200 bikes you'll find elsewhere. What's the real constraint here?
pairs.negative: I understand but $400 is the price and I'm not going to lower it just because you say it's outside your budget. Every buyer says that to try to get a discount and I've heard it a hundred times before. If you can't afford it, save up and come back later when you have the money. The bike will probably still be here.

pairs.context: You're buying a washer listed at $350 on Craigslist. The seller says they need to sell it by this weekend.
pairs.positive: You mentioned needing to sell by this weekend — what's driving that timeline? If you're moving, I might be able to help with the logistics of getting it out of your place quickly, which would save you time and effort on top of the sale. If it's about needing cash for something specific by a certain date, I can do an electronic transfer today so you have the money immediately. Tell me what the real urgency is and I'll see how I can make the deal work for both of us.
pairs.negative: If you need to sell by this weekend you're probably going to have to take whatever offer comes your way, which means my offer of $200 is actually pretty good given your time pressure. You don't have the luxury of waiting for a higher offer and the clock is ticking on your deadline. $200 cash right now and your problem is solved — take it or gamble on finding someone better by Saturday.

pairs.context: You're selling a camera for $450 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I need to think about it.'
pairs.positive: Totally fair — what specifically are you weighing in the decision? Is it the price, or are you comparing this to another camera model that might work better for your needs? If it's about price, I might have some flexibility depending on what you'd want to pair with it. If it's about whether this is the right camera for you, tell me what you plan to shoot and I can help you figure out if this body and lens combo actually matches your use case.
pairs.negative: Well, while you're thinking about it someone else might buy it — I've had six inquiries this week and interest is high. The price is $450 and it's a great deal for what you're getting at this quality level. I'd suggest making a decision quickly before you lose out on it entirely. Let me know by tomorrow or I'm moving to the next buyer in line.

pairs.context: You're buying a TV listed at $400 on Craigslist. The seller won't go below $375.
pairs.positive: I hear you on $375 — but help me understand what matters to you about this sale besides the dollar amount. If a clean, fast transaction is important, I can be at your door in an hour with cash and I'll carry it out myself so you don't have to lift a finger. If reducing hassle is valuable to you, that speed and ease of sale might be worth meeting me at $350 even though it's $25 less. What would make this the easiest sale you've ever done on Craigslist?
pairs.negative: My max is $325 and I'm not going to $375 no matter what you say about the TV. I've already told you my budget and it's not changing, so either drop the price or I'll find another TV tonight. There are plenty of listings and I'm not married to this particular one by any means.

pairs.context: You're selling a desk for $200 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I found a similar one for $150 from another seller.'
pairs.positive: That's helpful context — what makes you still interested in mine if the other one is cheaper? Is there something about this desk that the $150 one doesn't have — maybe the size, the material, the condition? Understanding what drew you to my listing despite the price difference tells me what you actually value, and maybe we can find a price that reflects that. If the other desk truly checks all the same boxes, I'd understand going with it.
pairs.negative: Then go buy that one for $150 — nobody is stopping you from getting the cheaper desk if that's what you want. My desk is $200 and I priced it based on what it's worth, not based on what other people charge for their stuff. I'm not going to price-match every random listing on the internet.

pairs.context: You're buying gym equipment listed at $600 on Craigslist. You don't need everything in the set.
pairs.positive: Before we talk price, let me understand what you need versus what I'm looking for — you might want to keep some items and I might not need others. Which pieces are most important for you to sell? If the dumbbells are the hardest to move separately, maybe I take those plus the bench and we find a price that works. The goal is to figure out a split where you sell what you need to sell and I only buy what I'll actually use.
pairs.negative: I only want the squat rack and the bench, so I'll offer $300 for just those two pieces. I'm not paying for dumbbells and plates I don't need and won't use. If you want to sell it all together find someone else, but I'm offering $300 for what I want. Take it or leave it.

pairs.context: You're selling a guitar for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I can get a new entry-level guitar for the same price.'
pairs.positive: You could, and for some players that's the right choice — so what's making you consider a used guitar at the same price point instead of buying new? If it's because you want better build quality and tone than what entry-level offers, this guitar gives you that since it's a $600 instrument at half price. If you're not sure about committing to guitar long-term, maybe the new cheaper option makes more sense. What's your actual goal — to learn casually or to have an instrument you'll play for years?
pairs.negative: A new entry-level guitar is trash compared to this — the wood is cheap, the hardware is flimsy, and it sounds like a toy. This is a $600 guitar that I'm selling for $300 which is objectively a better deal than buying garbage from a big box store. You'd be making a huge mistake buying new at this price point and anyone who plays guitar would tell you the same thing.

pairs.context: You're buying a patio set listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller seems emotionally attached to it.
pairs.positive: I can tell this set means something to you beyond just the money, and I'd love to hear the story behind it. Is it important to you that it goes to someone who'll use it and take care of it, or are you more focused on getting the best price for it? If it matters that the buyer appreciates it, I should tell you this would be for our first backyard patio and we'd be hosting family dinners on it every weekend. Knowing what matters to you beyond price helps me make an offer that actually works for both of us.
pairs.negative: Look, I don't care about the history of the furniture, I just want to buy a patio set at a good price. It's used furniture and the emotional value doesn't add any dollars to what it's worth on the market. My offer is $300 cash and I can pick it up today. Are you selling it or keeping it?

pairs.context: You're selling a stroller for $175 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'That's a lot for a used stroller. $100.'
pairs.positive: I understand the hesitation — what's making you feel $175 is too much? Is it because you've seen used strollers listed for less, or is $100 just what fits your current budget? If it's a budget constraint, I might be able to include the rain cover, cup holder attachment, and car seat adapter at $150 to give you more value without spending extra later. If you're comparing prices, this model retails for $450 new and the folding mechanism alone sets it apart from anything under $300.
pairs.negative: These strollers sell for $150-$200 used all day long, so $100 is a lowball and I'm not going there. I know what it's worth and I'm not going to let someone talk me into giving it away for half price. $175 is fair and final — everyone on Facebook Marketplace is listing the same model for more than I am. You're actually getting a deal at $175.

dimensions.name: Emotional Regulation
dimensions.description: Maintaining composure and professionalism when challenged, provoked, or under pressure; not letting emotions drive responses

pairs.context: You're selling a laptop for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'That's a complete scam. You're trying to rip people off with this overpriced garbage.'
pairs.positive: I understand the price might feel high at first glance, but let me walk through the specs and condition so you can see where the value is. The laptop has 16GB RAM, a 512GB SSD, and an i7 processor — comparable machines on eBay are going for $475-$550 right now. I'm open to discussing a fair price if you're genuinely interested after reviewing the details. If it's not in your budget, that's completely fine too and I wish you luck finding what you're looking for.
pairs.negative: Are you kidding me? Scam? I spent $1,200 on this laptop and $500 is already a massive discount for what you're getting. Don't come at me calling things a scam when you clearly can't even be bothered to research what these cost. Get out of my inbox and go waste someone else's time with your insults.

pairs.context: You're buying a car for $8,000 on Craigslist. The seller yells: 'You're insulting me with that lowball offer!'
pairs.positive: I'm sorry the offer felt insulting — that genuinely wasn't my intention and I'd like to find a number that works for both of us. I based my offer of $6,500 on the KBB value for this year, make, and mileage, so it comes from data rather than disrespect. Let me share the comps I looked at so we can talk about this with the same information in front of us. I think we can find middle ground if we focus on what the market data actually shows.
pairs.negative: Insulting? You're the one who listed a car with 120,000 miles for $8,000 like it's some kind of bargain. Maybe if you did five minutes of research you'd know your car isn't worth anywhere near what you're asking for it. Don't get angry at me for knowing the market better than you do.

pairs.context: You're selling a couch for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer no-showed, wasting your afternoon, then messages asking to reschedule.
pairs.positive: I cleared my afternoon to be here, so I'll be honest — the no-show was frustrating and I'd rather not have a repeat of that experience. That said, I'm still willing to sell the couch if you're genuinely interested and committed this time. If we reschedule, I need a confirmed time and a heads-up text an hour before you arrive to make sure we're both still on track. Can you commit to that for Saturday?
pairs.negative: Seriously? You wasted my ENTIRE Saturday afternoon sitting around waiting for someone who never showed up and didn't even bother to text. Now you want to reschedule like nothing happened? No thanks — find another couch from someone who doesn't value their time. I'm done dealing with flaky people like you.

pairs.context: You're buying a bike for $350 on Craigslist. The seller suddenly demands $450 because another buyer offered more.
pairs.positive: I understand you got a higher offer, which puts you in a good position as a seller and I respect that. That said, we agreed on $350 earlier and I've already arranged a truck to come pick it up tomorrow based on that agreement. I'd like to honor the original price we discussed, but if you feel you need to go with the higher offer, I understand and I'll look elsewhere. What would you like to do?
pairs.negative: Oh so now you're going to jack up the price after we already agreed? That's incredibly dishonest and I should have known better than to trust a random Craigslist seller. You people always pull this bait-and-switch garbage and it's infuriating every single time. Forget the bike — I wouldn't buy from someone this shady even if you dropped it to $200.

pairs.context: You're selling a TV for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer shows up, inspects it, and says: 'This looks way worse in person. I'll give you $100.'
pairs.positive: I appreciate you coming to look at it in person — that's always the best way to make a decision on used electronics. The condition matches exactly what I described and photographed in the listing, so I'm not sure what you were expecting to see differently. I'm not able to do $100, but if there's a specific concern about the condition I'm happy to address it and discuss a fair adjustment. What specifically looks different to you compared to the photos?
pairs.negative: Way worse? Are you blind? The TV looks exactly like the photos I posted and you knew what you were getting before you drove over here. This is just a cheap tactic to lowball me in person after I wasted my time waiting for you to show up. $100 is insulting and you should be embarrassed for even saying that number out loud.

pairs.context: You're buying a desk for $200 on Craigslist. You've been messaging the seller for a week and they keep changing their terms.
pairs.positive: I've noticed the terms have shifted a few times in our conversations — first $200 with delivery, then $200 pickup only, and now $225. I want to make sure we lock down the specifics so there are no more surprises for either of us. My offer is $200 with me handling pickup, which is what we originally agreed on last Tuesday. Can we finalize those terms right now so we can both move forward with confidence?
pairs.negative: I am so done with this runaround — every single message you change the deal and move the goalposts on me. Do you even know what you want? First it was one price, then another, and now you're adding even more on top. I've wasted an entire week on this and I should have walked away after the second flip.

pairs.context: You're selling a washer for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer sends angry texts after you sold it to someone else who arrived first.
pairs.positive: I understand you're frustrated and I genuinely feel bad about the timing — the other buyer showed up with cash before your scheduled appointment and the listing was first-come-first-served. I should have texted you right away to let you know, and I apologize for not communicating that sooner. If it helps, I know someone else selling a comparable washer in the area and I'm happy to connect you with them. Again, sorry about how that played out.
pairs.negative: Not my problem that you took too long to come get it — first come, first served is how Craigslist works and everyone knows that. Stop blowing up my phone about it because the washer is gone and your anger isn't going to bring it back. Maybe next time don't wait three days to come look at something if you actually want it.

pairs.context: You're buying a camera for $400 on Craigslist. You discover mid-conversation that the seller lied about the shutter count being low.
pairs.positive: I just checked the shutter count from the test shot I took and it shows 85,000 actuations — you mentioned it was around 15,000 in our messages, which is a significant difference that changes the value considerably. I'd like to give you the chance to explain in case there's a misunderstanding with the count method or an error somewhere. If 85,000 is accurate, I'd need to adjust my offer to $250 to reflect the actual wear. Can you clarify what happened with that number?
pairs.negative: You straight up lied to me about the shutter count — 85,000 is nothing close to 15,000 and you know it. I can't believe I drove thirty minutes to look at a camera from someone who deliberately misrepresents their stuff. This is exactly why Craigslist has such a terrible reputation — because of dishonest sellers like you.

pairs.context: You're selling a guitar for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer brings a friend who aggressively nitpicks everything wrong with the guitar.
pairs.positive: I appreciate the thorough inspection — having an experienced friend check things out is smart and I'd do the same thing buying used. Let me address each point: the fret buzz on the 7th fret is a truss rod adjustment that takes five minutes, and the scratch on the back is cosmetic only and doesn't affect tone or playability. I could come down to $275 to account for the fret work needed, which would cost about $30 at a shop. Does that address the concerns your friend raised?
pairs.negative: Tell your friend to stop pretending he's a guitar expert — he's been finding problems with everything for the last fifteen minutes and half of what he's saying isn't even accurate. This guitar plays great and sounds great and I'm not going to stand here being lectured by some random guy who probably can't even play. Either buy it for $300 or both of you can leave.

pairs.context: You're buying a patio set for $400 on Craigslist. The seller makes a condescending remark about your budget being too low.
pairs.positive: I can see we have different views on the value, and that's fine — these conversations work best when both sides feel respected. My offer of $300 is based on what I've seen similar sets sell for locally, and I've included the links in a message below so you can see my reasoning. I'm not trying to undervalue your furniture and I'd like to find a number we both feel good about. If $300 is genuinely too low, tell me where you need to be and I'll see if I can meet you somewhere closer.
pairs.negative: Condescending about my budget? You're selling used outdoor furniture on Craigslist, not auctioning art at Sotheby's — get over yourself. If $300 is beneath your dignity then sit on your overpriced patio set alone all summer while nobody buys it. I was trying to be reasonable but clearly that's wasted on someone with your attitude.

pairs.context: You're selling a fridge for $450 on Craigslist. The buyer accuses you of hiding a dent they claim wasn't in the photos.
pairs.positive: Let me pull up my original listing photos right now so we can look at them together — I took close-up shots of every side precisely to avoid this kind of misunderstanding. If the dent is visible in the photos, then it was disclosed and the price already reflects it. If it's genuinely not in the photos and I missed it, then I acknowledge the oversight and I'm willing to adjust the price by $30-$40 to account for it. Can we look at the photos together and figure this out?
pairs.negative: I didn't hide anything — you must need glasses if you couldn't see that in the photos I posted. Every detail was right there in the listing for anyone with functioning eyesight to see before they drove over. Don't accuse me of being dishonest when you're the one who can't be bothered to look at photos carefully.

pairs.context: You're buying a snowboard for $350 on Craigslist. After agreeing on a price, the seller ghosts you for five days then offers it to you at a higher price.
pairs.positive: I noticed it's been five days since we agreed on $300, and now you're asking $350 — I want to understand what changed on your end since we last spoke. If you got a better offer during that time, I'd appreciate you being upfront about it so I can decide whether to match or move on. I'd still like the board at $300 since that's what we agreed to, but I understand if circumstances changed. What happened?
pairs.negative: Five days of silence and now you come back trying to squeeze more money out of me? That is incredibly disrespectful of my time and honestly it's borderline dishonest behavior. I've been turning down other boards waiting on you like an idiot. Forget the whole thing — I'll never buy from someone this unreliable.

dimensions.name: Strategic Concession-Making
dimensions.description: Making concessions conditionally, reciprocally, and with decreasing magnitude; never giving something for nothing

pairs.context: You're selling a MacBook for $800 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'Can you come down on the price at all?'
pairs.positive: I can come down to $750, but only if you can pick it up today and pay cash — that saves me the hassle of scheduling around more buyers this weekend. If you want delivery or need to wait until next week, the price stays at $800 because that flexibility costs me time. The $50 discount is my way of rewarding a quick, clean transaction. Does today work for you?
pairs.negative: Sure, I can drop it to $650 — I've had this listed for a while and I just want it gone at this point honestly. I know $800 was probably too high to begin with and I should have priced it lower from the start. If $650 still feels high, tell me what works for you and I'm sure I can make it happen. DEAL=650

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $9,000 on Craigslist. The seller dropped to $8,500 but you want $8,000.
pairs.positive: I appreciate you coming down to $8,500 — that's a real move and I want to reciprocate in good faith by coming up from $7,500 to $8,000. That $500 gap is where we need to get creative: if you include the floor mats and the roof rack, I'll close at $8,250 right now and we both walk away with something extra. I won't go higher than $8,250 and that's already $750 above where I started this negotiation. What do you say?
pairs.negative: Ok I'll just do $8,500 then — I really like this car and I don't want to lose it over $500 when I've been searching for months. You're right that it's in good condition and the price is fair when I think about it compared to dealer prices. Let me come get it tomorrow and we'll close this out. DEAL=8500

pairs.context: You're selling a couch for $600 on Craigslist. You've already dropped from $600 to $550. The buyer says: 'Can you go any lower?'
pairs.positive: I've already moved $50 from my listing price, which is a meaningful concession on a $600 couch. Before I consider moving further, I need you to come up from where you are — what's your current best offer? I might have a small amount of room left, but I'm only moving if you do first because right now I've been the only one making concessions. Fair is fair.
pairs.negative: Yeah, I suppose I can go a bit lower since I really want to get this sold before the weekend. How about $475? Or actually, would $450 work better for you? I just don't want this to drag out any longer and I want to clear the space in my living room. Let me know what you think and I'll probably say yes. DEAL=450

pairs.context: You're buying a bike for $400 on Craigslist. The seller won't include the helmet and lock you asked about.
pairs.positive: If you include the helmet and lock, I'll pay the full $400 listing price without any further negotiation on the bike itself. The helmet and lock together might be worth $50-$60 used, but you'd have to list them separately and deal with more buyers, so bundling them with me saves you that hassle entirely. I'm trading convenience for accessories — we both get something out of it. Does that bundle work at $400?
pairs.negative: Ok that's fine, I'll buy the bike at $400 without the helmet and lock. I guess I can just buy those separately from somewhere else later even though it'll cost more. I don't want to push too hard and risk losing the bike over some accessories. DEAL=400

pairs.context: You're selling a TV for $350 on Craigslist. You dropped from $350 to $325. The buyer asks: 'Will you do $275?'
pairs.positive: I can do $300, and that's my final number — I've already come down $50 from the listing price and $300 is a strong deal for a 2023 QLED in this condition. But at $300, I need you to pick it up tonight because I have another buyer lined up for Saturday at $325 who I've been putting off. This is a one-time offer that expires when you leave this conversation. DEAL=300 if you commit right now.
pairs.negative: Hmm, $275... well, I really need the money this week and you're the only buyer who's actually followed through on coming to see it. I guess $275 is close enough to what I wanted and I'd rather have the cash now than keep relisting and hoping. Ok fine, $275 it is — when can you come get it? DEAL=275

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $250 on Craigslist. You offered $180 and the seller countered at $230.
pairs.positive: I appreciate you coming down from $250 to $230 — I'll come up from $180 to $200 as a matching gesture since you moved $20 and I'm moving $20 as well. For me to go any higher than $200, I'd need you to throw in the desk lamp you mentioned having — that way the extra money has extra value behind it. I won't go above $210 even with the lamp, so we're getting close to where I cap out. Does $200 with the lamp included work?
pairs.negative: You know what, $230 is only $30 more than I originally wanted to pay and I'm tired of going back and forth on this. Life's too short to argue over $30 when the desk is exactly what I need for my home office setup. I'll just pay the $230 and pick it up after work tomorrow. DEAL=230

pairs.context: You're selling a washer for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer wants you to deliver it for free.
pairs.positive: I'll deliver it, but the total goes up to $330 to cover my gas and the hour of time it takes me to load, drive, and unload. Alternatively, you can pick it up yourself and keep the price at $300 — that's the tradeoff and it's your choice which option works better for your situation. What I'm not going to do is deliver a heavy appliance for free when it costs me real time and gas money to do it. Which option do you prefer?
pairs.negative: I mean, I guess I can deliver it since I have a truck and it's not that far from where I live. I don't want to lose the sale over delivery when I've been trying to sell this washer for two weeks already. I'll bring it over this weekend and we'll keep it at $300 so everyone's happy. DEAL=300

pairs.context: You're buying a camera listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller dropped to $475. You want $400.
pairs.positive: I appreciate the $25 drop from $500 to $475 — let me match your concession proportionally by coming up from $400 to $425. That puts us $50 apart and I think we can close at $450 if you include the extra battery and the 64GB SD card you mentioned having. I'm making each concession smaller and conditional because I'm getting close to my ceiling here. $450 with the accessories is a clean deal for both of us.
pairs.negative: I guess $475 isn't that far from what I wanted and this camera is exactly the model I've been searching for everywhere.
