I keep talking myself into spending more but at this point I should just pull the trigger and stop overthinking it. Ok, $475 — I'll come get it tonight with cash. DEAL=475

pairs.context: You're selling gym equipment for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer already got you down to $550 and asks for free delivery.
pairs.positive: I've already discounted $50 from the listing price, so I'm not adding free delivery on top of that — each concession has to come with something in return. If you want delivery, the price goes back up to $575 to cover the cost of renting a truck since this stuff won't fit in my car. Or you can pick it up at $550 and save yourself the $25 — your call. I've already given you the price break so the delivery needs to be a separate transaction.
pairs.negative: Well, I already said I'd do $550 so I guess free delivery on top of that isn't a huge deal — I have a buddy with a truck who owes me a favor anyway. I just want this equipment out of my garage because it's taking up too much space and I need it gone. I'll bring it over Saturday morning. DEAL=550

pairs.context: You're buying a patio set listed at $500 on Craigslist. You've gone from $350 to $375 and the seller dropped from $500 to $450.
pairs.positive: We've both moved — you came down $50 from $500 to $450, and I came up $25 from $350 to $375 — so we're $75 apart right now. I'll come up one more time to $400, but that's my final move and each step I take is getting smaller for a reason. For me to hit $400, I need you to include the umbrella and cushion covers since those add value without costing you cash. $400 with the accessories and I'll pick up today. Final offer.
pairs.negative: You've been pretty firm at $450 and I respect that you know what your furniture is worth. I've been lowballing you honestly and $450 is actually a decent price for a teak patio set in this condition when I look at it objectively. I'll just pay the $450 — I'm tired of going back and forth when we're this close. DEAL=450

pairs.context: You're selling a guitar for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer wants you to include new strings and a setup.
pairs.positive: I'll include the setup if you pay $300 without any further price negotiation — the setup costs me about 20 minutes of work but saves you $50 at a shop, so you're getting real value. The strings I'll replace with new ones only if you commit right now and pick up today, because I'm not doing that work for a maybe. Each thing I add costs me time or money, so the deal stays at $300 when I'm including extras. Does $300 with setup and new strings work if you come tonight?
pairs.negative: Sure, I'll throw in new strings and do a quick setup on it — I want you to be happy with the guitar and have a good experience buying it from me. Actually, I can probably come down on the price a little too since you seem like a nice person who'll appreciate the instrument. How about $250 with the setup and strings? That seems like a fair deal all around. DEAL=250

pairs.context: You're buying a fridge listed at $450 on Craigslist. The seller dropped from $450 to $425. You're at $350.
pairs.positive: You've moved $25 from your listing price, so I'll move $25 from mine — I'm now at $375. To go any higher than that, I need something extra in return: either delivery to my place or including the extra water filter you mentioned having for the fridge. Moving from $375 to my absolute ceiling of $400 requires that trade because I'm running out of room to concede here. $400 with delivery or $375 with pickup — what works for you?
pairs.negative: The fridge looks really nice in person and $425 is honestly not that far from what I planned to spend. I've been shopping around for two weeks and this is the best condition fridge I've seen at any price point in the area. Let me just stop being cheap about it and pay $425 so I can finally have a working fridge at home. DEAL=425

dimensions.name: Anchoring
dimensions.description: Setting advantageous reference points early in a negotiation; establishing the range in which discussion takes place

pairs.context: You're buying a used Honda Civic listed at $9,000 on Craigslist. The seller asks what price you had in mind.
pairs.positive: Based on KBB and the last 30 days of comparable Civic sales in this zip code, fair market value for this year, mileage, and condition is $6,200 to $6,800. I'd offer $6,500, which is right in the middle of that data-driven range. I've got the screenshots of recent sales to back that up if you want to see them. That's my starting point based on what the market says, not what I wish the price was.
pairs.negative: I'm not really sure what to offer — what's the lowest you'd consider? I don't want to insult you with something too low or overpay either. Maybe you could give me a range of what you're thinking and I'll see if it fits my budget. Whatever you think is fair is probably close to what I'd be comfortable with.

pairs.context: You're selling a MacBook Pro listed at $900 on Craigslist. The buyer asks: 'What's your best price?'
pairs.positive: My best price starts with context: this same model sells refurbished from Apple for $1,399, and used on eBay the average sold price is $950-$1,050 in comparable condition. At $900, I'm already $50-$150 below the typical used selling price before we even start negotiating. I'm pricing it to sell but I know exactly what it's worth, so $900 is the number and there's data behind it. I can show you the eBay sold listings if that helps.
pairs.negative: Oh, I don't know, what's your budget? I listed it at $900 but I'm definitely open to offers and I'm pretty flexible on the price honestly. I just want to find a fair number that works for both of us. Make me an offer and we'll go from there — I'm sure we can figure something out.

pairs.context: You're buying a couch listed at $700 on Craigslist. You want to establish a lower price range right away.
pairs.positive: I've been tracking similar couches on Craigslist and Marketplace for the past three weeks and the ones that actually sell — not just list, but sell — go for $300 to $450 in this area. Several are still sitting at $600+ with no buyers, which tells me the market ceiling is around $450 for used upholstered furniture of this type. My offer based on actual completed transactions is $375 — that's realistic for both of us based on what people are actually paying.
pairs.negative: I'm interested in the couch but I'm not sure what to offer — $700 seems like a lot but you probably know better than me what it's worth. Would you consider going a little lower? I don't want to lowball you. Maybe somewhere in the $500-$600 range? What do you think would be reasonable for both of us?

pairs.context: You're selling a PS5 bundle for $400 on Craigslist. A buyer messages asking about the price before making their offer.
pairs.positive: Here's the math on why $400 is actually a deal: a new PS5 is $500, the two extra controllers are $70 each new, and the six games retail for $30-$60 each. Total new value is over $850 for everything in this bundle, so at $400 you're getting the entire package for less than half of retail. I'm anchoring you to those real numbers because the value of this bundle is not $400 — it's $850 worth of stuff for $400. Keep that in mind before making an offer.
pairs.negative: I listed it at $400 but honestly I'm not sure if that's too high or too low — I kind of just picked a number. If you have a different price in mind I'd love to hear it. PS5 prices are all over the place right now and I don't really know what the market is doing. Just throw out a number and we'll work from there.

pairs.context: You're buying a dining table listed at $500 on Craigslist. You want to anchor low with comparable data.
pairs.positive: I've looked at the last two weeks of sold listings for similar dining tables and I found seven that sold: $225, $250, $275, $300, $300, $325, and $350. The average is $289 and the highest was $350, so the data puts fair value for this style and condition somewhere in the $275-$350 range. My offer is $300, which is actually at the higher end of what these tables are actually selling for. I'm being generous based on the numbers.
pairs.negative: I don't know much about furniture pricing so I'm not sure where to start with an offer. You probably have a better sense of what dining tables go for than I do. Would you maybe come down to like $400? Or is that too low? I'm open to paying more if you think $400 isn't fair.

pairs.context: You're selling a bike for $450 on Craigslist. The buyer says they saw a similar one listed for $250.
pairs.positive: That $250 listing is probably a different model year or a significantly lower component group — let me explain why mine is worth nearly double. This bike has a carbon fork, Shimano 105 groupset, and hydraulic disc brakes which are the three most expensive components on any road bike. A similar spec bike new from a shop costs $1,800-$2,200, so at $450 I'm pricing at 20-25% of new, which is the standard for a well-maintained, two-year-old bike with premium components. That $250 bike likely has none of those features.
pairs.negative: Oh really, $250 for a similar bike? I didn't know they were going that cheap. Maybe my price is too high — I'll come down to $300 to be more competitive with the other listing. I just want to sell it and I don't want to be overpriced compared to what's out there on the market right now. Would $300 work for you?

pairs.context: You're buying a washer listed at $400 on Craigslist. You want to set the negotiation range before the seller does.
pairs.positive: Before we talk price, I want to share what I've found: this model has a 4.1 rating on Consumer Reports with a typical used price range of $175-$250 for a unit this age. The last three that sold locally went for $200, $225, and $190 based on completed listings I saved screenshots of. My offer is $200, which is right in line with what the market data shows for this specific model and age bracket. I'd love to hear why you think yours is worth more than those recent sales.
pairs.negative: I see you have it listed at $400 — is there any wiggle room? I'm not sure what these washers are going for but $400 feels like it might be on the high side. What would you be willing to take for it? I don't want to start too low and offend you.

pairs.context: You're selling a camera for $550 on Craigslist. The buyer asks for your reasoning on the price.
pairs.positive: The reasoning is straightforward: this camera body sells for $450-$500 used on KEH and MPB, which are the two most reputable used camera dealers. I'm including a $200 lens and a $60 bag, so the total package value based on third-party dealer prices is $710-$760. At $550 for the complete kit, I'm offering a 25-30% discount compared to buying each piece separately from trusted dealers. You can verify all of those numbers yourself in about two minutes of searching.
pairs.negative: Honestly I just kind of looked around online and $550 seemed about right, but I'm not married to that number. If you have a different price in mind I'm all ears and very open to negotiation. I'm not a photography expert when it comes to pricing gear so I could be off.

pairs.context: You're buying a TV listed at $400 on Craigslist. You want to anchor the negotiation around depreciation data.
pairs.positive: TVs depreciate roughly 30-40% in the first year and 50-60% by year two based on the resale data I've tracked across hundreds of listings. This is a two-year-old model that retailed at $700, which puts the fair used value at $280-$350 using standard depreciation rates. I'm offering $300, which is actually at the higher end of that depreciation-based range. The math is straightforward and I'm happy to show you the data that supports this price.
pairs.negative: I'm not sure what a two-year-old TV should cost used — would you take $350 maybe? Or is that too low? I saw your listing at $400 and I'm just not sure if that's what these go for normally. Feel free to tell me what you think is a good price because I don't have a strong number in mind.

pairs.context: You're selling a set of tools for $400 on Craigslist. A buyer questions your pricing.
pairs.positive: Let me break down the value: the table saw alone retails for $350 new at Home Depot and mine has less than 20 hours of use on it. The orbital sander is $120 new, the drill press is $275, and the miscellaneous hand tools total about $150 in new value. That's $895 of tools I'm selling for $400, which is a 55% discount from buying everything new individually. You'd struggle to find a comparable bundle for less than $500 anywhere online, so $400 is already priced to move.
pairs.negative: I'm asking $400 for the set but I'm not totally sure if that's fair or not since I haven't sold tools before. If you think it should be less I'm open to hearing what you'd pay for everything. I don't want to be greedy — just make an offer and we'll see if it works for both of us.

pairs.context: You're buying gym equipment listed at $700 on Craigslist. You want to anchor by pointing to a flooded market.
pairs.positive: I counted 23 active listings for comparable home gym setups within 30 miles of us right now — the market is flooded with COVID gym equipment that people don't use anymore and that drives prices down significantly. The median asking price for those 23 listings is $425, and the ones that actually sold averaged $375. My offer of $400 is above the average selling price, which makes it generous given how much supply is out there. The data strongly suggests $400-$450 is where these sets are moving right now.
pairs.negative: I'm interested in the equipment but $700 is more than I expected. What's the least you'd accept for everything? I've seen some gym setups online but I'm not sure how prices compare for this specific equipment. I'm flexible on budget — just want to find a price that makes sense.

pairs.context: You're selling a leather jacket for $200 on Craigslist. A buyer seems interested but hasn't made an offer yet.
pairs.positive: Before you make an offer, know that this is a Schott NYC jacket that retails for $850 and has a waiting list at most retailers — you literally cannot buy this jacket new right now without waiting months. Used Schott jackets in this condition sell on Grailed and eBay for $400-$550 based on the last 10 completed transactions I checked. At $200, I'm pricing this at less than half of the typical used resale, which is why it won't last long at this price. I'd suggest acting quickly.
pairs.negative: I have the jacket listed at $200 — what do you think, is that about right? I'm really not sure what used leather jackets go for these days and I might be pricing it wrong in either direction. Let me know what you'd pay for it and I'll be flexible.

dimensions.name: Rapport Building
dimensions.description: Establishing trust, connection, and a positive relationship with the counterpart to facilitate cooperative problem-solving

pairs.context: You're buying a guitar listed at $300 on Craigslist. The seller mentions in the listing that they've been playing for 20 years.
pairs.positive: Twenty years of playing — that's amazing, you must have some incredible stories and experiences from your time with this guitar. What kind of music did you mainly play on it? I'm getting back into playing after a long break and something about this guitar's tone in your video really grabbed me. I want to make sure it goes to someone who'll play it regularly, and I promise this one would be my daily player if we can work out a fair price for both of us.
pairs.negative: I want to buy this guitar. What's the lowest price you'll accept for it? I don't need the backstory — just the price and when I can pick it up. Let's keep this simple and transactional since we're both busy people. $200.

pairs.context: You're selling a car for $8,000 on Craigslist. The buyer mentions they just moved to the area.
pairs.positive: Welcome to the neighborhood! Moving to a new city is exciting but also a lot — how are you settling in so far? This car has been great for getting around the area and I'm happy to point you to a trustworthy mechanic nearby for future maintenance if you end up buying it. I'd like to find a fair price that starts your time here on a good note rather than feeling like someone took advantage of you being new in town. How about we start at $7,500 and see where we land?
pairs.negative: The car is $8,000 firm. I don't really care where you moved from or why you're buying — the price is based on the market value and condition, period. Let me know if you want it at that price and we can schedule a time for you to come inspect it. $8,000 cash, no negotiations.

pairs.context: You're buying a bike listed at $400 on Craigslist. The seller's listing mentions they customized it themselves.
pairs.positive: I can tell from the listing that you put real thought into the customization — the handlebar tape color matching the saddle is a nice touch that most people wouldn't bother with. What parts did you upgrade and what was your vision for the build? I'm asking because I appreciate a bike that was put together with intention rather than just slapped together with whatever was cheap. If the build quality is as good as it looks, I'm happy to pay a fair price that reflects the care you put into it.
pairs.negative: I'll offer $250 for the bike. I don't care about the customization or the colors — I just need something to ride and this is available in my area. Let me know if that price works and what times are available for pickup this week.

pairs.context: You're selling a bookshelf for $150 on Craigslist. The buyer mentions it's for their kid's room.
pairs.positive: That's great — a well-organized bookshelf can really help a kid develop a love of reading, which is one of the best gifts you can give them honestly. My own kids outgrew this one but it held up to years of books being shoved in and pulled out by little hands, so it's definitely kid-durable. I've got some bookends that match the finish if you're interested — I could include those free of charge since they'd complete the look. How about $135 for the shelf and I'll throw in the bookends as a bonus for being a thoughtful parent?
pairs.negative: The bookshelf is $150. It's in the condition shown in the photos and available for pickup anytime. I don't need to know what it's for or who it's for. Are you buying it at $150 or not? I have other inquiries I need to respond to today.

pairs.context: You're buying a coffee table listed at $200 on Craigslist. You notice in the photos that the seller has great taste in decor.
pairs.positive: I have to say, your living room setup in the background of those photos looks incredible — you clearly have a great eye for design and that gives me confidence this table was well-chosen and well-cared for. The mid-century style is exactly what my partner and I have been searching for to tie our living room together. We've been furnishing our first home together and this would be a real centerpiece for the room. I'd like to offer $175 — and if you ever sell that lamp in the corner, let me know because I'm interested in that too!
pairs.negative: I want the table. $120. When can I get it? I need something for my living room and this fits the space. Let me know the pickup details.

pairs.context: You're selling a vintage record player for $275 on Craigslist. The buyer is clearly excited about vinyl in their messages.
pairs.positive: I can tell you're genuinely passionate about vinyl — that's awesome because this turntable deserves to go to someone who appreciates the analog experience rather than just flipping it for profit. I've had incredible listening sessions with this player and it really brings out warmth in the sound that digital just can't replicate. If you end up buying it, I'd recommend the album 'Kind of Blue' as your first spin because the bass tone on this turntable with that record is magical. How about $250 since I know you'll actually use it and love it?
pairs.negative: The record player is $275 and it works. I don't use it anymore and just want the cash. If you want it, come get it with $275 in hand. I'm not interested in discussing vinyl or music — just the transaction.

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $175 on Craigslist. The seller mentions they work from home.
pairs.positive: Another work-from-homer! I've been at it for three years now and a good desk makes such a huge difference for productivity and posture when you're sitting there eight hours a day. What do you do for work, if you don't mind me asking? I'm curious what kind of work this desk has supported — and more practically, how does it hold up over long work sessions in terms of wobble and comfort? I'm looking to offer $150, and if it's as solid as it looks, this could be the beginning of my home office upgrade starting with the most important piece.
pairs.negative: I'll give you $100 for the desk. I work from home and need something functional. Let me know if that works and what time I can come get it. I prefer to keep Craigslist transactions quick and businesslike.

pairs.context: You're selling a kayak for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer mentions they're new to kayaking.
pairs.positive: You're going to love kayaking — there's nothing quite like being out on the water with just you, the paddle, and the sound of nature around you. This kayak is actually perfect for beginners because it has a wider hull for stability and a comfortable seat that you can adjust as you learn what position works best. I took it out on Lake Cascade about fifty times and never once tipped it, which says a lot about how forgiving it is for new paddlers. How about $375, and I'll throw in my old paddle and life jacket to get you started with everything you need on day one?
pairs.negative: Kayak is $400 and I'm firm on the price. I don't care if you're a beginner or a professional — the kayak doesn't change price based on the buyer's experience level. Cash only, pick it up from my garage. If you need accessories buy them separately at a sporting goods store.

pairs.context: You're buying a washer/dryer listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller seems like a fellow parent based on the kids' stuff visible in photos.
pairs.positive: I noticed the toys in the background of your photos — fellow parent life, I see! Between our three kids and the dog, our old washer finally gave up trying to keep up with the endless laundry mountain. How has this set handled the family workload? We go through about two loads a day on busy weeks and I need something that can take that beating without complaining. I'm thinking $425 is fair — and as one laundry-drowning parent to another, I genuinely appreciate you keeping these appliances in good shape despite the chaos of family life.
pairs.negative: I'll offer $350 for the washer/dryer. I need reliable appliances and these look functional from the photos. When is pickup available? I'd like to schedule something for this weekend and keep the process efficient.

pairs.context: You're selling camping gear for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer says it would be for their first camping trip.
pairs.positive: First camping trip — you're in for an amazing experience! There's something about sleeping under the stars and waking up to fresh mountain air that just resets your whole brain. This gear set has been on over thirty trips with me and has held up through rain, wind, and one very memorable encounter with a curious raccoon. I'd love to see this stuff go to someone starting their outdoor adventure rather than sitting in my garage. How about $260, and I'll walk you through how to set everything up so your first trip goes smoothly?
pairs.negative: The camping gear is $300 for the lot. Everything is listed in the description and it all functions as described in the photos. Let me know if you want to buy it and when you can come pick it up. First time, last time, doesn't matter — $300.

pairs.context: You're buying a dining set listed at $350 on Craigslist. The seller mentions they're downsizing after the kids left for college.
pairs.positive: Empty nest — that's a big transition! Our oldest just started looking at colleges and the thought of the house getting quieter is already hitting me. I can imagine this table has seen a lot of family meals, homework sessions, and holiday gatherings over the years, so I'll make sure it continues that tradition in our home. We have game nights every Friday that need exactly this kind of sturdy table. I think $300 is a fair price — and I genuinely hope the downsizing brings you some exciting new adventures with all that extra space and freedom.
pairs.negative: I need a table and chairs. $200 for the set. I can pick it up whenever works for you. Let me know if that price is acceptable.

pairs.context: You're selling a telescope for $250 on Craigslist. The buyer mentions they want to stargaze with their grandchildren.
pairs.positive: That is such a wonderful idea — I got into astronomy exactly the same way, through my grandfather pointing out constellations in the backyard when I was about eight years old. Those memories stick with you forever and this telescope is perfect for sharing that experience because it's powerful enough to see Saturn's rings but simple enough for kids to use with a little help. I set it up in my yard last week and Jupiter's moons were crystal clear even from the suburbs. How about $225, and I'll include the star chart I used when I was learning — your grandkids might enjoy it.
pairs.negative: The telescope is $250 firm. It works as described and shown in the listing photos. I don't care what you use it for — $250, cash, and you pick it up. Let me know if that works.

dimensions.name: BATNA Awareness
dimensions.description: Understanding your best alternative to a negotiated agreement; being willing to walk away; not being desperate to close

pairs.context: You're selling a couch for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: '$250 take it or leave it.'
pairs.positive: I'll leave it. I've got three other people scheduled to see the couch this weekend and my listing has been getting steady views at the current price point. $250 is half my asking price and well below what I've seen comparable couches sell for in this area recently. I'm comfortable waiting for the right buyer rather than taking a deal that undervalues what I'm selling. Good luck with your search though.
pairs.negative: Hmm, I don't have any other serious offers right now and $250 is at least something in my pocket. I've had the couch listed for three weeks and you're the first person who actually wants to come get it. I guess $250 is better than nothing and I really need the space in my apartment cleared out. Ok fine, let's just do it. DEAL=250

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $10,000 on Craigslist. The seller won't go below $9,500.
pairs.positive: I appreciate the negotiation but $9,500 doesn't work for my budget, especially when I've got a similar car lined up through a private sale at $8,200 with slightly lower mileage. Your car is nice but I'm not paying a $1,300 premium over my backup option when both vehicles are in comparable condition. I'd prefer yours, but not enough to overpay by that much when I have a solid alternative waiting. If you come down to $8,500 I'm very interested, otherwise I'll go with the other deal.
pairs.negative: I really love this specific car and I haven't seen another one like it in months of searching — it's exactly the color and trim I wanted. You're probably right that $9,500 is fair since it's so hard to find one this clean. I don't have any other real options and I'm afraid if I say no this one will be gone by the weekend. Ok, $9,500 it is. DEAL=9500

pairs.context: You listed a laptop for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer threatens to buy from someone else.
pairs.positive: That's completely fair and I hope you find a good deal wherever you end up buying. My laptop is priced at $600 because that's what the specs and condition support, and I've sold four laptops this year at similar price points without any trouble. I'm not worried about one buyer walking away because the listing consistently generates interest at this price. If you change your mind, my offer stands — but I'm not going to discount it out of fear of losing a single sale.
pairs.negative: Wait, please don't go to someone else — let me see what I can do on the price to keep you interested. I've had the laptop listed for a while and you're the first buyer who seemed really serious about purchasing it. How about $425? I really want to get this sold and I'd rather have a sure thing with you than start over with new listings. DEAL=425

pairs.context: You're buying a bike listed at $400 on Craigslist. The seller pressures you to decide immediately.
pairs.positive: I appreciate the urgency but I don't make purchase decisions under time pressure — that's a rule I have that has saved me money more times than I can count. I've got two other bikes to look at this week and I'll make my decision after seeing all three so I can compare condition, value, and ride quality fairly. If yours is the best option I'll come back with cash, but I'm not committing today just because you're pushing for it. I'll follow up by Thursday with my decision.
pairs.negative: Oh no, I don't want to lose this bike — you're right that I should just decide now before someone else buys it. I've been looking for weeks and this is the best one I've seen so far, so what's the point of looking at others. $400 is fine, let me just grab it right now before I overthink this. DEAL=400

pairs.context: You're selling a TV for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I can get a new one for $400 on sale.'
pairs.positive: Then that might be the better move for you — a brand new TV with a full warranty for $50 more is objectively a good deal and I wouldn't talk you out of it. My TV is priced at $350 because it's a higher-end model than what's on sale at $400, with better contrast ratio and HDR performance, but if those specs don't matter to you the new one wins on warranty alone. I'm comfortable with my listing price because someone who values these specific features will come along this week. No hard feelings either way.
pairs.negative: Oh wow, really? $400 for a new one? Maybe I should lower my price then — I don't want you to buy the new one when mine is right here and available. How about $250? That way you're saving $150 compared to the sale and getting a TV you can take home today. I really need to sell this soon and I'd hate to lose a buyer over $50 when I can just drop the price. DEAL=250

pairs.context: You're buying furniture on Craigslist and the seller says there are multiple interested buyers.
pairs.positive: That's great for you — competition usually means you've priced it well and the furniture is desirable, so congratulations on that. My offer is $400 and it stands until tomorrow at noon, after which I'll be committing to a different set I've been looking at from another seller. I'm not going to get into a bidding war because I have options too, and I'd rather pay a fair price than an inflated one driven by artificial urgency. Let me know your decision by tomorrow.
pairs.negative: Oh really, other buyers? I don't want to miss out on this since I've been looking for weeks and this is exactly what I want for my living room. How about I just pay full price right now to lock it in before someone else grabs it? I don't even need to negotiate — the full asking price is fine as long as you hold it for me. DEAL=600

pairs.context: You listed a gaming console for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'Nobody is paying $350 for a used console these days.'
pairs.positive: My sales data disagrees — I've sold three consoles this year at $325-$375 and all moved within a week of listing at those prices. The market for used consoles with game bundles is actually quite strong because buying everything separately new costs over $700. If nobody is paying $350 then I'll find out soon enough when my listing doesn't get offers, but so far I've had five inquiries this week. I'm confident enough in my price to be patient about it.
pairs.negative: Maybe you're right — the console market is probably changing and I haven't kept up with what they're actually selling for now. I haven't gotten as many responses as I expected on the listing, which maybe means $350 is too high. How about $200? I don't want it sitting around forever and I'd rather get some money for it than none. DEAL=200

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $200 on Craigslist. The seller says it's their absolute final price.
pairs.positive: I respect that, and I want you to know I'm not trying to grind you down — $200 just doesn't work for me when I can get a similar desk from two other sellers for $140-$160 in comparable condition. I'm not bluffing about those alternatives and I'm happy to share the listings with you so you can see what I'm comparing against. If $175 works for you I'll come get it today, but if $200 is truly firm, I'll wish you luck and go with one of the other options this weekend. No hard feelings either way.
pairs.negative: I guess $200 is $200 and I should just accept it since I've been shopping around forever and I'm exhausted from looking at desks. The other ones I've seen aren't as nice as this one and I'd probably regret passing on it just to save $30 or $40. I don't have the energy to keep searching. Fine, $200 works, when can I come by? DEAL=200

pairs.context: You're selling a washer for $300 on Craigslist. A buyer offers $150 and says they'll walk if you don't accept.
pairs.positive: $150 doesn't work for me, and I'm fine with you walking — I'd rather keep the washer listed at $300 than sell it for half price under pressure from a single buyer. My listing has been active for four days and gets consistent views, and I'm in no rush to sell since the washer works great and I'm not moving anytime soon. If your budget changes, the offer to buy at $275 is open through the weekend. Otherwise, best of luck finding what you need elsewhere.
pairs.negative: Please don't walk away — $150 is really low but maybe we can find some middle ground before you go? I'm nervous that nobody else is going to offer more and I've been wanting to sell this for a while now. What if I did $175? Or even $160? I just really don't want to lose a buyer when you're right here ready to pay. DEAL=160

pairs.context: You're buying a camera for $450 on Craigslist. The seller is pressuring you to pay full price immediately.
pairs.positive: I don't make $450 decisions under pressure and I have a standing offer on a comparable camera body for $375 from another seller who's been very patient with my timeline. I'd prefer your camera because the lens inclusion is a nice bonus, but not enough to overpay by $100 when my alternative is solid. My offer is $375 for the body and lens — if that works, great; if not, I'm comfortable going with my backup option by the end of this week. Take your time deciding.
pairs.negative: You're right, I should just commit before someone else gets it — I've been wanting this exact camera model forever and it would be devastating to miss it over some haggling. I don't really have another camera lined up and who knows when another one pops up for sale at this price point. $450 is fine, I'll make it work somehow. DEAL=450

pairs.context: You listed tools for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'Last offer — $200. Take it or I'm done.'
pairs.positive: Then we're done, and I genuinely mean that without any bitterness about it. $200 is half my asking price and these tools are worth every penny of $400 based on what they'd cost to replace individually at any hardware store. I can relist them next month if they don't sell this month, and I'll still come out ahead waiting versus taking $200 now. Thanks for your interest and I hope you find the tools you need at the price you're looking for.
pairs.negative: Wait, $200 is better than nothing and I really need the cash this week for some bills coming due. I probably should have listed them for less from the start anyway since they've been sitting in my garage for years collecting dust. You're right that $200 is fair enough since at least I get something for them. Ok, deal. DEAL=200

pairs.context: You're buying a fridge for $500 on Craigslist. The seller says several people are interested.
pairs.positive: Good for them — competition is healthy and it means you have a quality product worth fighting over. My offer is $400 and it comes with the advantage of picking up today with a truck and crew ready to load it, which none of your other buyers can guarantee right now. If someone offers more, take their money and I'll move to the LG I've been eyeing on another listing at $350 — I have options too and I'm not going to panic-buy because of other buyers. Let me know by 5pm today.
pairs.negative: Other people are interested? Oh no, I really need a fridge and this one is perfect for my kitchen layout. I don't want to risk losing it by haggling when someone else might just come in and pay full price while I'm trying to negotiate. I'll pay the full $500 right now, please just hold it for me and don't sell it to anyone else. DEAL=500

dimensions.name: Reframing
dimensions.description: Shifting the narrative, perspective, or framing of a problem to open new possibilities for agreement

pairs.context: You're selling a laptop for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'That's way too expensive for a used laptop.'
pairs.positive: Instead of thinking of it as a used laptop, consider what you're actually getting: a machine that does everything a $1,200 new one does at half the cost, with zero setup time because everything is already configured and ready to use out of the box. The 'used' label saves you $600 and a trip to the store — that's not a downside, that's the whole point. If you were buying a car, you wouldn't hesitate to buy certified pre-owned to save money, and this is the same concept. $600 for $1,200 worth of performance is a smart purchase, not an expensive one.
pairs.negative: I understand it's expensive but that's just what these laptops cost. The specs are good and the condition is excellent so $600 is the price I need. I know used electronics are tricky but this one is in really good shape. If $600 is too much for your budget then I understand.

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $12,000 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'I already dropped the price from $14,000.'
pairs.positive: I appreciate the $2,000 reduction, but let me reframe how I'm looking at this: the question isn't how much you've dropped from your original price — it's whether $12,000 is the right price for this car based on where the market is today. If comparable cars are selling at $9,000-$10,000, then $12,000 is still above market regardless of where you started. The anchor that matters is the market value, not the original listing price. Based on that framing, I think $9,500 is where we should be.
pairs.negative: I guess you've already come down a lot from $14,000, so maybe $12,000 is actually pretty reasonable when I look at it that way. You did make a big concession and I should appreciate that rather than asking for more off the price. $12,000 it is, since you've already been generous about lowering it. DEAL=12000

pairs.context: You're selling a couch for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I'm not sure I want to pay $500 for something that might not match my room.'
pairs.positive: Think about it this way: you're not just buying a couch, you're saving yourself weeks of shopping at furniture stores where you'd pay $1,500-$2,000 for comparable quality and wait months for delivery on top of that. If it doesn't match perfectly, you could easily resell it on Craigslist for close to what you paid since used furniture holds value at this quality level. The real risk isn't spending $500 on something that might not match — it's spending months searching for perfection and sitting on the floor in the meantime.
pairs.negative: I can understand the uncertainty about matching — it's hard to tell from photos how things will look in your actual space. If you're not sure about it, I totally get it and maybe you should keep looking for something you feel more confident about. The couch is $500 if you want it but I don't want to pressure you into something you're not comfortable with.

pairs.context: You're buying a desk for $200 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'I spent $800 on this desk just two years ago.'
pairs.positive: I hear you on the $800 investment, but let me offer a different way to think about it: you got two years of daily use from that desk, which works out to about $1.10 per day of value you've already extracted. Selling it now for $150 means your total cost of ownership was only $650 for two years of use — that's actually a great deal compared to renting furniture or buying cheap desks that break every year. The $150 isn't a loss, it's recovering value from something that already paid for itself through use.
pairs.negative: You're right, $800 is a lot to spend on a desk and I can see why $200 feels low compared to what you paid. Maybe I should pay closer to what you're asking since it's clearly a quality piece. I don't want you to feel like you're taking a big loss on it. How about $200 since that's what you're asking?

pairs.context: You're selling a bike for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'I can buy a new bike for $500.'
pairs.positive: For $500 you'd get a brand new bike with the cheapest components they could put on it — generic brakes, basic gears, and heavy wheels that you'll want to upgrade within six months. For $400 you're getting a bike with components that would cost $1,200 on a new bike, already broken in so everything shifts and brakes smoothly from day one. The question isn't new versus used — it's whether you want entry-level new parts or mid-range proven parts. You're actually buying better performance for less money if you think about it that way.
pairs.negative: I understand the new bike appeal — there's something nice about having something brand new out of the box that nobody else has ridden. If you think the new bike is a better option for you, I completely understand that decision. My bike is $400 and it's a good bike but I know new is tempting.

pairs.context: You're buying a washer listed at $350 on Craigslist. The seller says the price is firm because it's barely been used.
pairs.positive: Actually, barely used might not be the selling point you think it is — a washer that's sat unused for long periods can develop seal issues, gasket drying, and stale water smell that's expensive to fix. A washer that's been run regularly stays lubricated and working properly because the seals stay flexible and the drum stays clean. So 'barely used' actually introduces risk for me as a buyer, which means $350 should come down to $275 to account for the potential maintenance I might need to do after purchase.
pairs.negative: Barely used definitely makes it worth more — I can see why you're firm at $350 when it hasn't had much wear and tear from heavy use. A washer that's like new is practically as good as buying from the store. I think $350 is fair for something in that condition. DEAL=350

pairs.context: You're selling a camera for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'Smartphone cameras are so good now, why would I pay $500 for a dedicated camera?'
pairs.positive: That's actually the perfect reason to buy this camera — smartphones have gotten good enough that people now understand photography and want to take the next step, which is exactly where a dedicated camera changes everything. Try shooting in low light, controlling depth of field, or printing a 20x30 poster from your phone versus this camera — there's no comparison and you already know that or you wouldn't be looking at camera listings. Think of this as a creative tool that unlocks capabilities your phone physically cannot achieve, not as a redundant device.
pairs.negative: You have a point — phone cameras have gotten really impressive and they're so convenient to carry around everywhere you go. Honestly a lot of people probably don't need a dedicated camera anymore unless they're really serious about photography. If you're on the fence, maybe a phone is enough for your needs. The camera is $500 if you want it though.

pairs.context: You're buying a TV listed at $400 on Craigslist. The seller says they need $400 because they're buying a bigger one.
pairs.positive: I understand needing funds for the upgrade, but let me reframe the math: if you sell this TV quickly at $350, you have money in hand this week to jump on a deal for the bigger TV. If you hold firm at $400 and it takes three more weeks to sell, you've lost three weeks of enjoying your new TV and might miss the deal you're eyeing while waiting. Sometimes accepting $50 less now is worth more than $50 extra two weeks from now. My offer of $350 today gives you buying power this afternoon.
pairs.negative: That makes sense — you need $400 to fund the upgrade and I don't want to stand in the way of you getting the TV you actually want. It would be selfish of me to try to drive the price down when you have a specific financial need for the full amount. $400 is fine. DEAL=400

pairs.context: You're selling a guitar for $300 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'There are cheaper guitars on the market.'
pairs.positive: There are cheaper guitars the same way there are cheaper cars — but you wouldn't compare a Honda Civic to a bicycle just because both have wheels. The cheaper guitars use laminated wood that sounds flat and dies acoustically within a few years, while this one has a solid spruce top that actually improves in tone the more you play it. Buying cheap means buying twice: you'll outgrow a $100 guitar in six months and end up spending $300-$400 on your second guitar anyway. Buying this one now saves you the throwaway purchase.
pairs.negative: You're right, there are definitely cheaper options out there if price is the main factor for you. I can see how $300 seems like a lot when there are guitars available for half that or less. If budget is your primary concern, one of those cheaper models might make more sense for where you are right now. My guitar is $300 if you decide it's worth it.

pairs.context: You're buying furniture listed at $700 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'I'm already losing money at this price.'
pairs.positive: I understand the feeling of loss, but consider it from an ownership perspective: you bought this furniture to use it, and you've gotten years of daily value from it. The money wasn't lost — it was spent on furnishing your home, and now you're recovering a portion of that investment rather than donating it or paying someone to haul it away. At $500, you're still recouping a significant chunk while I'm getting quality furniture at a fair used price. Reframing from 'losing money' to 'recovering value' makes $500 feel much more reasonable.
pairs.negative: I don't want you to lose money on the deal — that doesn't feel right. Maybe $700 is actually fair since you clearly paid a lot more for it originally. I should respect what you've invested in the furniture rather than trying to lowball you. Let me just pay $700. DEAL=700

pairs.context: You're selling patio furniture for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer says: 'Winter is coming, I won't even use it for six months.'
pairs.positive: That's actually exactly why now is the smartest time to buy patio furniture — you're getting a $500 set at end-of-season pricing that would cost you $700-$800 if you waited until spring when demand spikes and everyone is shopping. Think of the six months as free storage in your garage while your investment appreciates relative to the market. Smart shoppers buy winter coats in summer and patio furniture in fall — you're doing this exactly right and $500 now saves you hundreds versus what you'd pay in April.
pairs.negative: Yeah, the timing is admittedly not ideal for patio furniture since you'd be storing it all winter without using it. I probably should have listed this back in June when people were actually in buying mode for outdoor furniture. Maybe I should wait until spring to sell it or lower the price to compensate for the seasonal timing.

pairs.context: You're buying a standing desk listed at $250 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'These are very popular, everyone wants standing desks now.'
pairs.positive: Actually, the popularity is exactly why prices have come down — when everyone was buying standing desks during COVID, used ones were scarce and expensive. Now the market is flooded with used standing desks because people who bought them are going back to offices and don't need them anymore. High supply plus lower demand means prices should be below pre-COVID levels, not at the same elevated level. Based on the current supply-demand dynamic, $175 is where these are actually selling, not $250.
pairs.negative: You're right, standing desks are really popular and lots of people want them for health benefits and better posture. If demand is high then $250 is probably fair since you could easily sell it to someone else who wants one. I should grab this while it's available. DEAL=250

dimensions.name: Patience
dimensions.description: Taking time before responding, resisting pressure to rush, and using silence as an effective negotiation tool

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $10,000 on Craigslist. The seller says: 'I need an answer right now, I have someone else coming to look at it in an hour.'
pairs.positive: I understand there's another buyer coming, but I'm not going to make a $10,000 decision in the next sixty minutes because that's how people end up overpaying for things they regret. I'd rather lose this car than rush into a purchase I haven't fully thought through — there will always be another car on the market. If you're still available tomorrow, I'll give you a firm answer after I've slept on it and reviewed my budget. Good luck with the other buyer in the meantime.
pairs.negative: An hour? Ok, ok — I don't want to lose this car, so let me just say yes right now. I haven't finished researching the market price or getting a mechanic to inspect it, but I'm too worried about someone else snatching it to wait any longer. $10,000 works, let me just do it before I lose my chance. DEAL=10000

pairs.context: You're selling a laptop for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer makes an offer and waits for your immediate response.
pairs.positive: Thanks for the offer — let me sit with it overnight and get back to you tomorrow morning with a definitive answer one way or the other. I have a few other inquiries I want to weigh against yours to make sure I'm making the right decision for myself. Taking a day to think never hurts anyone and it means whatever answer I give you will be solid and committed. I'll message you by 10am tomorrow with either a yes, a no, or a counter.
pairs.negative: Ok um, $450? That's lower than I wanted but I'm getting nervous that if I say no you'll move on and then I'll have nobody interested in the laptop. You know what, let's just do it at $450 before I talk myself out of it or you change your mind. DEAL=450

pairs.context: You're buying a couch listed at $500 on Craigslist. The seller is pushing hard for an immediate deposit to hold it.
pairs.positive: I don't send deposits on Craigslist and I don't make rushed financial decisions, even on a couch I really like. Here's what I will do: I'll come see it Saturday at 10am, and if it matches the photos and listing description, I'll pay full price in cash on the spot. That gives you certainty of sale and gives me certainty of quality, and we both have until Saturday to feel good about it. Patience benefits both sides here.
pairs.negative: I'm worried if I don't send a deposit right now, someone else will come along and buy it before I get the chance. Let me just send you $100 to hold it even though I haven't seen it in person yet. I'd rather risk the $100 than risk losing this couch entirely because I waited too long to act. Can you send me your payment info?

pairs.context: You're selling a TV for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer lowballs you at $150 and asks: 'Yes or no?'
pairs.positive: I'm not going to give you a rushed yes or no on an offer that's less than half my asking price — that doesn't deserve an immediate answer, it deserves a thoughtful counter. Give me until tomorrow and I'll come back with the lowest price I'd accept, which will be well above $150 but might be lower than $350 depending on how the other conversations I'm having go. Rushing me into a bad deal isn't going to work, but patience might get you a better price than you'd expect.
pairs.negative: Wow, $150? That's so low... but you want an answer right now and I'm panicking because I might not get another offer this week. What if you leave and nobody else wants the TV? Ok, how about $200? Actually, $175? I just want to make this happen before you walk away and I'm left with nothing. DEAL=175

pairs.context: You're buying a bike listed at $400 on Craigslist. It's a great deal but you haven't test-ridden it yet.
pairs.positive: I can see this is a good bike at a fair price, but I'm not buying any bike I haven't ridden for at least fifteen minutes first. I'd like to come by on Wednesday, take it for a proper test ride around the neighborhood, and if everything feels right mechanically, I'll hand you $400 cash on the spot. Good deals come and go, but buyer's remorse from rushing is worse than missing out on any single bike. Are you available Wednesday evening?
pairs.negative: This looks perfect and $400 is such a good price that I don't even need to ride it first — I can tell from the photos it's exactly what I want and I don't want to risk it being sold to someone else while I schedule a test ride. I'll take it right now, sight unseen if I have to, just hold it for me and I'll come with cash today. DEAL=400

pairs.context: You're selling a camera for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer makes an initial offer after five minutes of chatting.
pairs.positive: I appreciate the quick offer, but I've only had this listed for two days and I want to give the market a full week to respond before accepting anything below asking price. Early offers are often the lowest offers, and I'd rather be patient and see what comes in over the next five days than rush into the first deal that appears. If your offer of $400 is still on the table Friday, I'll seriously consider it — but right now I'm going to let the listing breathe.
pairs.negative: Well, you're the first person to actually make a real offer so maybe I should just take it before you lose interest and move on. $400 isn't what I wanted but it's here right now and who knows if anyone else will offer more this week. I'll just go with your offer since a bird in the hand is better than two in the bush. DEAL=400

pairs.context: You're buying a washer listed at $300 on Craigslist. The seller texts 'still interested?' after you didn't reply for a day.
pairs.positive: Yes, still interested — I took a day to research this specific model's reliability ratings, common issues, and fair market price so I can make an informed offer rather than guessing. After doing that homework, I'd like to offer $225 based on what I found about typical resale values and the age of the unit. Taking a day to research benefits us both because my offer is now backed by data instead of feelings. Is $225 in your range?
pairs.negative: Oh sorry, I didn't mean to leave you hanging! Yes I'm still interested, I was just busy and lost track of my messages. I don't want you to think I flaked out or that I'm not serious about buying it. $300 is fine — let me come grab it before you sell it to someone else. When works for pickup? DEAL=300

pairs.context: You're selling a desk for $200 on Craigslist. The buyer asks if you'd take $100 and needs to know immediately.
pairs.positive: I'm not responding to urgency-driven offers — if the deal is good today, it'll be good tomorrow too and there's no reason either of us needs to rush this decision. $100 is well below what I'm looking for, and I'd need to think about my absolute minimum floor before countering that. Give me until Thursday and I'll come back with a number that I've properly thought through. If you need a desk before Thursday, I understand if you buy elsewhere.
pairs.negative: You need to know right now? Ugh, the pressure is getting to me — I've been trying to sell this desk for a week and nobody else has even offered anything. $100 is really low but it's the only money on the table and what if nobody else comes along? Fine, I'll take it before I miss even this opportunity. DEAL=100

pairs.context: You're buying a TV for $350 on Craigslist. You're tempted but want to compare prices first.
pairs.positive: The TV looks great and I'm very interested, but I have a personal rule: I never buy the first thing I see in a price range without checking at least two alternatives first. I've bookmarked two other TVs to compare this weekend, and if yours is the best value — which it might be — I'll come back Monday ready to buy at a fair price. Smart shopping takes a little patience but it means I'll be fully committed when I do make an offer.
pairs.negative: I know I should probably shop around and compare a few more TVs before committing, but honestly I'm afraid this one will sell over the weekend while I'm looking at other options. You know what, I'm just going to buy it now so I don't have to worry about losing it. $350 is fine. DEAL=350

pairs.context: You're selling gym equipment for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer makes a lowball offer and says they're walking out the door to buy from someone else.
pairs.positive: If you have a better deal lined up, I'd genuinely encourage you to take it — no hard feelings and I mean that sincerely. I'm not going to drop my price to $300 because someone threatens to leave, since that kind of pressure negotiation means I'd always sell at a loss. My equipment will sell at $600 because it's quality gear at a fair price, and whether that happens today or next month doesn't change the value. The door is always open if you come back.
pairs.negative: Wait, don't leave! What if I dropped to $350? I can see you're serious about the other seller and I really don't want to lose this sale when you're literally standing here ready to buy. Maybe $300 even? I just want to sell this stuff and clear out my garage before the weekend. DEAL=300

pairs.context: You're buying a guitar for $300 on Craigslist. The listing just went up an hour ago and you're the first to inquire.
pairs.positive: I see you just posted this — I'm interested but I want to schedule a time to come play it for twenty minutes before making any decisions on price. New listings sometimes get a flurry of interest that settles down after a day, so I'm not worried about missing out by taking my time to do this properly. Can I come by tomorrow evening to try it? If it plays well and we can agree on a price after I've tested it, I'll bring cash ready to buy.
pairs.negative: You just listed this? I want it right now before someone else sees it and scoops it up first. $300 is fine, I don't even need to play it — the photos look good and I trust your description. Can I come get it right now with cash? I don't want to risk waiting. DEAL=300

pairs.context: You're selling a fridge for $450 on Craigslist. Negotiations have stalled at a $75 gap between you and the buyer.
pairs.positive: We're $75 apart and I think the best move for both of us right now is to step away from this conversation for a couple of days and come back with fresh eyes. Sometimes a little distance from a negotiation helps both sides figure out what they're actually comfortable with. I'll revisit my bottom line over the weekend and you can think about yours, and we'll reconnect Monday to see if we can bridge that gap. No pressure, no deadlines — just two people taking time to make a good decision.
pairs.negative: We've been going back and forth on this $75 gap for too long and I just want it resolved right now, one way or the other. I'm splitting the difference — $37.50 off my price — just to end this negotiation because the uncertainty is killing me. Let's close at $412.50 before I change my mind again. DEAL=412

dimensions.name: Value Creation
dimensions.description: Expanding the set of possible agreements by trading across multiple issues; finding win-win outcomes beyond simple compromise

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $8,000 on Craigslist. You can only afford $7,000 but the seller won't budge.
pairs.positive: What if we think beyond just the sticker price? You mentioned needing help moving boxes next weekend — I have a truck and a free Saturday, so I could help you move in exchange for a $500 price reduction. That saves you $300-$400 on a moving service and I get the car at $7,500, which is a win we couldn't find by just haggling over the number. Sometimes the best deal isn't about the price — it's about trading things that cost one side less than they're worth to the other.
pairs.negative: I can only afford $7,000 and you want $8,000, so I guess we're stuck and there's nothing to discuss further. It's a simple math problem — the numbers don't work and I can't change my budget. Sorry we couldn't make it happen.

pairs.context: You're selling a furniture set for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer says: '$600 all at once is more than I can swing right now.'
pairs.positive: What if we break this into two transactions that work better for your cash flow? Take the dining table and chairs now for $350 and come back for the buffet table next month at $250. That way you start using the pieces you need most immediately and I get the full $600 over time instead of discounting to make a single sale work. We both end up better off than if I just dropped the price or you walked away from the whole set.
pairs.negative: I understand $600 is a lot at once but that's the price for the full set and I can't split it up into separate purchases. Either you buy everything together for $600 or we don't have a deal — it's all or nothing because I want to clear everything out in one trip.

pairs.context: You're buying a laptop listed at $700 on Craigslist. You and the seller are stuck $100 apart.
pairs.positive: We're $100 apart on the laptop price, but I noticed you mentioned having an external monitor you're not using anymore. What if I buy the laptop at $650 and the monitor for $75 — you get $725 total which is more than your asking price, and I get a complete setup worth over $1,000 for less than I'd pay buying everything separately. By expanding the deal to include the monitor, we actually created $25 of extra value for you and saved me money compared to buying each item from different sellers.
pairs.negative: I can't go above $600 and you want $700 — we're too far apart and there's no way to bridge a $100 gap on a single laptop purchase. I appreciate your time but the math just doesn't work out for me at that price.

pairs.context: You're selling a washer/dryer set for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer only needs the washer.
pairs.positive: I'd prefer to sell both together, but here's an idea: take the full set for $400 — that's $100 off — and resell the dryer yourself for $150-$200 on Craigslist. You end up getting the washer for effectively $200-$250 after reselling the dryer, which is way less than buying a washer alone, and I get a clean single-transaction sale. You make money on the flip and I avoid the hassle of splitting the set and managing two separate buyer conversations.
pairs.negative: I'm only selling them as a set for $500. I don't want to split them up because then I'm stuck with a dryer that's harder to sell on its own. You either take both for $500 or I'll wait for someone who wants the pair. That's the only option I'm offering.

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $200 on Craigslist. The seller won't deliver and you don't have a truck.
pairs.positive: I don't have a truck, but here's what I can offer instead of asking you to deliver: I'll come Saturday with my buddy who has a pickup, and we'll disassemble and carry everything out ourselves so you don't have to lift a finger. In return for handling all the labor, I'd like the price at $175 instead of $200. You save the physical effort and I save $25 — we're both trading something that costs us less than what we get in return. That's a better deal than either of us just conceding on price.
pairs.negative: I can't pick it up because I don't have a truck and you can't deliver, so I guess this deal isn't going to work out for either of us. It's a logistical problem that can't really be solved — sorry for wasting your time.

pairs.context: You're selling a camera for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer wants it for $400 and you can't go below $450.
pairs.positive: Let me suggest something that might work: I'll do $450 for the camera and I'll throw in a two-hour photography lesson since I've been shooting professionally for eight years. You'd pay $100-$200 for a lesson from any local photographer, so you're getting $550-$650 worth of value for $450. I'm sharing knowledge that costs me nothing but time, and you're getting something that makes the camera ten times more useful to you from day one. That way $450 feels like a steal rather than a stretch.
pairs.negative: My minimum is $450 and your max is $400 — we're $50 apart with no way to close the gap on a single camera transaction. I wish I could help but I can't go lower than $450 and if your budget is $400 then I think we're at an impasse.

pairs.context: You're buying a TV listed at $400 on Craigslist. You're $50 apart and stuck.
pairs.positive: We're stuck on the number, so let me think about this differently: what if I pick up the TV tonight instead of Saturday, which means you don't have to keep it listed and deal with more messages all week? I'd also take the old TV stand you mentioned wanting to get rid of — that saves you a dump run or donation trip. Quick sale plus trash removal might be worth $50 to you, and I get the TV at $350 plus a stand I needed anyway. We both win on things that don't cost us cash.
pairs.negative: $350 is my number and $400 is yours. I've been thinking about it and I just don't see how we close a $50 gap when we've both been firm on our positions. This is just a straightforward price disagreement with no solution.

pairs.context: You're selling gym equipment for $600 on Craigslist. The buyer wants everything for $400.
pairs.positive: What if I keep the kettlebells — which I might actually use — and you get the squat rack, bench, barbell, and plates for $425? I'm essentially giving you a $25 discount on the big items and keeping gear that has value to me, so I'm not just losing $175. Or, take everything at $475 and I'll help you set up the squat rack at your place, which normally takes two people and two hours. Either way we're trading across multiple dimensions instead of just fighting over one number.
pairs.negative: The full set is $600 and I can't break it apart or go down to $400. Either you pay $600 for everything or we don't have a deal. There's nothing creative we can do when you're offering $200 less than my asking price.

pairs.context: You're buying a guitar for $300 on Craigslist. You only have $250 in cash.
pairs.positive: I have $250 cash plus a guitar amplifier that I no longer need — it's a Fender Frontman worth about $75 used. If you're selling the guitar, you might not need the amp, but you could easily flip it for $60-$75 on Craigslist within a week. That gets you $310-$325 total value versus $300 in just cash, and I get the guitar within my cash budget. We're creating extra value by letting me trade something I don't need for something you can resell.
pairs.negative: I only have $250 and you want $300, which means I'm $50 short and there's nothing I can do about it. I can't magically create $50 I don't have. I'll have to pass on the guitar and keep looking for something in my price range.

pairs.context: You're selling a patio set for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer is worried about getting it home.
pairs.positive: I've got a truck and a free afternoon — what if I deliver the set to your place for an extra $30? That's less than half what a delivery service or U-Haul rental would cost you, and it means you don't have to borrow a truck or make two trips. At $530 with delivery, your total is still less than buying a comparable set new with shipping, and I make an extra $30 for an hour of my time. We just expanded the deal in a way that benefits both of us instead of fighting over the furniture price.
pairs.negative: I don't deliver — it's pickup only and you'll need to figure out your own transportation for getting it home. The set is $500 and that's the price at my location. If getting it home is too complicated for you, I understand if you need to pass.

pairs.context: You're buying a snowboard listed at $350 on Craigslist. You want boots too but the seller's boots are the wrong size.
pairs.positive: Your boots don't fit me, but here's a thought: I know someone selling size-11 boots for $80. What if I buy your board for $300 and you connect me with the boot seller, or better yet, what if you buy those boots for $80 and bundle them with the board for me at $375 total? You make $75 profit on the boots you didn't even own, I get a complete setup for $375 instead of spending $430 buying separately, and the boot seller moves their inventory. Everyone wins in a three-way deal.
pairs.negative: The boots don't fit so I'll just buy the board for $300. I'll have to figure out boots from somewhere else, which is annoying but it's a separate problem that can't really be solved in this transaction.

pairs.context: You're selling a fridge for $450 on Craigslist. The buyer says they also need a microwave.
pairs.positive: I actually have a microwave I was about to list for $75 — what if we bundle the fridge and microwave for $500 total? That saves you $25 versus buying them separately, saves me the hassle of creating another listing and dealing with a second buyer for the microwave, and gives you a one-stop shopping experience for your kitchen. Bundling lets me offer a discount I couldn't offer on either item alone because I'm saving time and effort on the second sale. Deal at $500 for both?
pairs.negative: I'm just selling the fridge, not the microwave. The fridge is $450 and that's a separate transaction from whatever else you need for your kitchen. I can't help with the microwave — you'll have to find that on your own.

dimensions.name: Information Gathering
dimensions.description: Asking probing, open-ended questions to understand the other party's constraints, priorities, and decision-making criteria

pairs.context: You're selling a car for $8,000 on Craigslist. A buyer reaches out saying they're interested.
pairs.positive: Great to hear you're interested! Before we talk price, I'd love to understand what you're looking for: What will you mainly use the car for — commuting, road trips, or both? Are there specific features that are must-haves for you? And is there a timeline you're working with for the purchase? The more I know about what matters to you, the better I can explain how this car fits — or doesn't fit — your needs. That way we both save time.
pairs.negative: Cool, the car is $8,000 and available for viewing this weekend. It runs great and has new tires. Let me know if you want to come see it and I'll give you the address. First come, first served.

pairs.context: You're buying a laptop listed at $600 on Craigslist. The seller seems motivated.
pairs.positive: Before I make an offer, I have a few questions: How long have you had the laptop listed, and how many serious inquiries have you gotten so far? What's driving the sale — are you upgrading, or do you need the cash for something specific? And is there a deadline you're working against? I'm asking because understanding your situation helps me make an offer that actually gets us both to yes instead of wasting time with back-and-forth on numbers that won't work for either of us.
pairs.negative: I'll offer $400 for the laptop. It looks good from the photos and the specs work for what I need. Let me know if that works and when I can pick it up.

pairs.context: You're selling a bike for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer asks: 'What's your lowest price?'
pairs.positive: Instead of jumping to a number, help me understand what you're comparing against: Have you been looking at other bikes in this price range? What's your budget for a bike, and does that include accessories like a helmet and lock, or is it just for the bike itself? And what kind of riding do you plan to do — casual weekend rides or daily commuting? Your answers help me figure out if this bike is actually the right fit for you, which matters more to me than just naming a bottom-dollar price.
pairs.negative: My lowest price is $375, take it or leave it. The bike is in great condition with new tires and recently serviced brakes. Let me know if you want it.

pairs.context: You're buying a couch listed at $500 on Craigslist. You want to know more before making an offer.
pairs.positive: The listing looks promising but I'd like to know more before coming to see it: How many people regularly used the couch, and was it in a main living space or a less-used room? Has anything been spilled on it or has it been professionally cleaned recently? And what's the reason for selling — are you upgrading, moving, or redecorating? These details help me figure out the real condition beyond photos and whether my offer should be at, above, or below the typical range for this style.
pairs.negative: Would you take $350 for the couch? I'm interested if the price is right. Let me know and I can come check it out this weekend.

pairs.context: You're selling a camera for $500 on Craigslist. A buyer asks one-word questions.
pairs.positive: I'd rather give you complete information upfront so we can have a productive conversation. Let me ask you a few things: What type of photography are you looking to do — portraits, landscape, street, or something else? Have you used a DSLR before, or would this be your first dedicated camera? And is the $500 price point your budget ceiling, or is there flexibility if the right package comes along? Understanding your goals helps me tell you whether this camera is actually the right match for what you're trying to accomplish.
pairs.negative: Yes it's available, yes it works, yes the lens is included. The price is $500 firm. Come look at it if you're interested and bring cash.

pairs.context: You're buying a washing machine listed at $350 on Craigslist. The listing is sparse on details.
pairs.positive: The listing doesn't have much info so I've got several questions before making an offer: What's the exact model number so I can look up specs and reliability reviews? How many years have you owned it, and roughly how many loads per week did you run? Has it ever needed repairs, and if so, what was fixed? Does it have any quirks like needing a second spin cycle or making unusual noises? These details tell me a lot more about value than the price alone, and I'd rather know everything upfront than discover surprises after purchase.
pairs.negative: Looks fine from the photos. Would you take $250? I need a washer and this one seems like it would work. Let me know if we have a deal.

pairs.context: You're selling a TV for $350 on Craigslist. The buyer seems unsure about what they want.
pairs.positive: It sounds like you're weighing your options, which is smart — let me help by asking a few things: What size room will the TV go in, and how far away from the screen will you typically sit? Do you mainly watch movies, sports, or use it for gaming? And is this replacing an old TV or going in a room that doesn't currently have one? Those answers will tell me whether this specific TV is right for your setup, because I'd rather help you find the right match — even if it's not mine — than sell you something you'll regret and want to return.
pairs.negative: The TV is a 55-inch Samsung QLED, $350 firm. It works perfectly and the picture is amazing. Interested?

pairs.context: You're buying a desk listed at $200 on Craigslist. You want to understand the seller's pricing rationale.
pairs.positive: Before I make a number, help me understand your thinking on the $200 price: How did you arrive at that figure — did you compare against similar desks online, or is it based on what you originally paid? How long has it been listed, and have you had other offers? And is $200 firm, or is there flexibility depending on how the pickup logistics work out? I'm not trying to lowball you — I'm trying to understand the logic behind the price so I can make an offer that reflects the same logic.
pairs.negative: I'll offer $120 for the desk. It's used and $200 seems high for what it is. Take it or leave it.

pairs.context: You're selling gym equipment for $600 on Craigslist. A buyer makes a quick lowball offer.
pairs.positive: Before we trade numbers, I'd like to understand what you're building: Are you setting up a full home gym, or just adding to existing equipment? What lifts are most important to you — squats, bench press, deadlifts? Do you have space constraints that limit what you can fit? And is $600 out of reach, or is it that you don't need everything in the set? Your answers help me figure out if selling the full set makes sense or if there's a smarter way to structure this for both of us.
pairs.negative: I'll take $350 for the lot. Gym equipment is everywhere on Craigslist right now and prices are way down from COVID. Let me know if $350 works.

pairs.context: You're buying a guitar listed at $300 on Craigslist. You want to understand condition beyond the listing photos.
pairs.positive: The photos look good but there's a lot that photos don't capture with guitars, so I have a few important questions: What's the fret condition — any flat spots, divots, or buzzing at specific frets? Has the truss rod been adjusted recently, and does the neck have any twist or bow that you've noticed? When were the strings last changed, and do you have the original case or is this a different one? And honestly, why are you selling — outgrew it, switching to electric, or just not playing anymore? All of this affects my offer significantly.
pairs.negative: Looks good from the pictures. I'll offer $200 — would that work for you? I can come try it out this week.

pairs.context: You're selling a patio set for $500 on Craigslist. The buyer hasn't provided much context about their interest.
pairs.positive: I'd love to help you figure out if this set is right for your space before we talk price: How big is your patio or deck area in terms of dimensions? Do you need the set to seat a specific number of people for entertaining? Is weather resistance a priority for you — do you plan to cover it or leave it out year-round? And are you comparing this against other sets right now, or is mine the main one you're looking at? These questions help me understand if I should be pitching the features or just negotiating the price.
pairs.negative: The patio set is $500 and includes everything shown in the photos. It's in good condition and available for pickup anytime. Let me know if you want it.
