pairs.context: You're buying a fridge listed at $500 on Craigslist. The listing says 'great condition' but you want specifics.
pairs.positive: I always dig a little deeper than 'great condition' before buying major appliances: What's the exact model and year of manufacture? Have both the compressor and ice maker been running without issues the entire time you've owned it? What's your average monthly energy cost with this fridge, roughly? Have you ever had any repair work done on it? And can I hear it running before I buy — are there any unusual sounds like clicking, humming, or cycling on and off frequently? I ask all of this because a $500 fridge that needs a $200 compressor isn't actually a $500 fridge.
pairs.negative: How about $350? That seems fair for a used fridge. I need one pretty soon so if you can do $350 I'll come this weekend.

dimensions.name: Clarity and Directness
dimensions.description: Communicating in clear, specific, unambiguous terms; making explicit asks and avoiding vague or hedging language

pairs.context: You're buying a car listed at $8,000 on Craigslist. You've decided on a price and want to make your offer.
pairs.positive: My offer is $7,200 cash. I'll pick it up Saturday at 10am from your driveway, bring the cash in hundreds, and we can complete the title transfer on the spot. I need a clear yes or no by Friday at 5pm so I can finalize my plans. If you counter, I'll consider anything between $7,200 and $7,800 but nothing above that. Those are my exact terms.
pairs.negative: I was kind of thinking maybe somewhere around, like, $7,000 or so? But I'm not totally sure, it could be more or less depending on how things look when I see it. I might be able to do more if the car is really nice in person. I don't know, what do you think? We could maybe figure out the timing later.

pairs.context: You're selling a laptop for $600 on Craigslist. A buyer asks about the condition.
pairs.positive: Here's the exact condition: the screen has zero dead pixels and no scratches, the battery holds 4.5 hours under normal use, the keyboard has a worn spot on the spacebar from heavy use, and there's a small dent on the bottom-left corner of the lid that doesn't affect function. Cosmetically I'd rate it 7 out of 10, functionally 9.5 out of 10. No defects are hidden — what I just described is everything. $600 reflects that overall condition.
pairs.negative: It's in pretty good shape overall, maybe a few small things here and there but nothing major that I can think of. The screen looks fine to me and the battery is decent. There might be a couple cosmetic things but they're not a big deal. It works well and I think $600 is fair for the condition it's in.

pairs.context: You're buying a couch listed at $500 on Craigslist. You want to schedule a time to see it.
pairs.positive: I'd like to see the couch Saturday at 11am at your place. I'll bring cash for the full amount in case I want to buy on the spot. I need exactly 15 minutes to inspect the fabric, sit on each cushion, and check the frame for structural issues. If anything doesn't match your listing description, I'll point it out immediately and adjust my offer accordingly. Does Saturday at 11am work?
pairs.negative: Maybe we could meet up sometime this weekend or maybe next week? I'm not sure about my schedule yet but I'll try to figure it out. I was thinking of maybe checking it out if I have some free time. I'll let you know when I know more about my plans.

pairs.context: You're selling a bike for $400 on Craigslist. The buyer asks what's included.
pairs.positive: The $400 includes: the bike frame and all components as shown in photos, a rear light with fresh batteries, and the bike lock attached to the seat post. It does not include: the helmet shown in photo three, the bike rack on my car, or the water bottle cage which I'm keeping for my other bike. No pedal wrench, no manual, no original packaging. What's listed is exactly what you get. Anything else you need, ask now.
